new_findings.title: Start at 50/50 split as baseline; negotiate higher % as skills improve

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Agencies that take creators from zero earn the right to keep a higher commission percentage because the agency is creating all the value; working with already-successful creators compresses margins.
variants.what_they_do: Jason ties commission rate directly to how much value the agency is creating relative to the creator. If the creator brings zero earning history, zero social following, and low talent, the agency justifies a higher-than-standard split because it is building everything from scratch. Conversely, signing a talented creator or one with existing revenue means accepting a compressed margin deal. He treats margin as a direct output of value-creation ratio.

sources.quote: you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline

as a result of our immense value creation, when working with creators, scaling them from absolute zero, we get to keep a higher percent than usual simply due to the fact that we are creating such an immense amount of value in the process.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding (§7) but has no documented split rationale, tiers, or progression logic. The baseline number Palm uses is not written down. This is flagged as §7's #1 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 50/50 as Palm's stated baseline in the onboarding contract and pitch deck. Document criteria for when Palm negotiates above 50% (e.g., creator already earning >$X/mo, Palm providing full chatting, paid traffic spend). Gives sales clarity and creator trust.

new_findings.title: Run a structured Canva onboarding presentation to kill scope creep at kickoff

variants.claim: A formal onboarding call presentation — covering process steps, revision limits, invoice terms, cancellation policy, and 'how to win' — eliminates scope creep and buyer remorse by setting hard expectations before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Build a Canva slide deck covering: welcome/team intro, reiterate buying decision with case studies, step-by-step delivery process with timelines, revision policy (2 revisions, batch feedback in one email), invoicing terms, meeting cancellation policy (72hr notice), additional services pricing, and monthly strategy call cadence. Present this at the kickoff call before any strategy discussion. Use it as a live walk-through shared on screen. Embed the deck in the client's Notion client OS for future reference.

sources.quote: any frustration is just an unmet expectation. And if we're not doing a good job at setting expectations, then the client's going to be frustrated
I like to use Canva to build this out. So, if I'm on the client call... we're just going to go over a quick onboarding presentation just to make sure we're really aligned on expectations
revisions. If you do have revisions, super happy to help. We limit those to two

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented onboarding call SOP or expectation-setting presentation for creators (§6 is thin). The creator onboarding form wizard collects info but there is no structured kickoff call protocol. This gap means scope creep and misaligned expectations are likely at creator onboarding.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm creator onboarding deck covering: what Palm does (social + content pipeline), creator responsibilities (filming, approvals), commission split and invoice cadence, content approval process and turnaround times, and escalation path. Embed in the creator-facing section of the admin portal or deliver via a recorded Loom. Assign to Josh or a future creator success role.

new_findings.title: Track 5 profit layers, not just creator gross revenue

variants.claim: Creator gross revenue is the least useful number for judging agency health; founders must track five sequential layers down to owner profit per hour.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan defines a 5-level waterfall: (1) creator gross, (2) agency net after split, (3) gross profit after fulfillment costs, (4) owner profit after everything, (5) owner profit per hour. He insists the only number that matters is what the founder actually keeps, and that 'revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash is reality.' He frames agencies claiming $100k/month as often netting $5-30k for the founder after chatters, managers, editors, VAs, software, promo, and mistakes are paid.

sources.quote: level two is the agency net revenue. So that is what you keep after the creator split
level four is owner profit. So this is what you take home after everything. This is the number that actually counts
level five is ultimately the one I would call the good metric. The owner profit per hour

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks creator gross via Inflow/FansMetric dashboards and computes agency net for invoicing (§7), but there is no documented gross-profit-per-creator, owner-profit, or owner-profit-per-hour metric. The invoicing system auto-computes splits and weekly PDFs, but stops at agency net — levels 3-5 of Dylan's waterfall are a GAP (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 'profit waterfall' view in the admin portal: creator gross → agency net (already computed) → gross profit (subtract per-creator fulfillment costs: chat team allocation, editor time, VA time, software) → owner profit (subtract fixed overhead) → owner profit per founder hour. Surface this weekly alongside the existing invoice dashboard. This is a high-priority instrumentation task given Palm's current THIN baseline on profitability metrics.

new_findings.topic: Three non-negotiable reel criteria: aesthetics, emotional curve, WTF moment
new_findings.title: Reject any reel missing aesthetics + emotional curve + pattern-interrupt — reshoot it

variants.claim: The difference between 6K-view and 6M-view reels is an engineered three-part formula: matching aesthetics to creator USP, a scripted look-away→look-in→smile emotional arc, and a deliberate stop-scroll pattern-interrupt moment.
variants.what_they_do: Aesthetics: outfit, location, lighting, framing all matched to the creator's visual USP (e.g., if USP is large chest + black hair, 80% of content angles highlight that — not hidden in hoodies). Emotional curve: reels are scripted with a 3-beat arc — look away (living her life) → look in (she noticed you) → smile (girlfriend moment) — engineered to create GFE in 3 seconds, repeated across the ~46 views a typical fan sees before subscribing. WTF moment: every reel has a deliberate background event (someone walking into a pole, a cat crossing) that breaks autopilot scrolling and triggers rewatches, which signals the algorithm.

sources.quote: Look away says she's living her life. She's not looking at you. Look in says she just noticed you watching. Then smile.
the emotional curve is the precise difference between a clip that gets 6,000 views and a clip that hits 6 million views
every reel has a stop scroll moment…something in the background, something natural, something the viewer didn't expect

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies *what* to film from viral data but does not codify *how* to film it at this granularity. The emotional curve and WTF moment criteria are absent from Palm's current documented production standards. Palm's editor workflow has approval gates but no stated rejection criteria at this level.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add these three criteria as a mandatory editor QA checklist in Palm's Task/approval workflow. For aesthetics: add a 'creator USP card' to each creator's Airtable record (visual USP, don'ts list) that editors reference on every clip review. For emotional curve and WTF moment: create a one-page shoot brief template with examples that goes to creators/videographers before every filming session. Clips failing any criterion should be flagged for reshoot, not edited around.

new_findings.topic: Vault structuring + custom hotkeys per creator
new_findings.title: Structure OF vault with labeled categories + creator-specific hotkeys to cut reply time to 50 seconds

variants.claim: A disorganized vault and generic hotkeys are the single largest hidden tax on chatter productivity; restructuring vault with categories and building custom per-creator hotkeys raised reply rates 60% in two weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Vault restructured within week 1: every content piece labeled by category (boy/girl, tier list, JOI, rapport building), exact price per video, and a defined script order (first PPV → second PPV → optional upsell → conditional branches). Chatters open CreatorHero, see the script order, copy-paste, send — 50-second reply time, no scrolling. Hotkeys are custom per creator: matched to the creator's speaking style, age, aesthetic, and specific vault content. Separate hotkeys for small talk, transitions, objection handling. The 18-year-old blonde's hotkeys use youth lingo; the milf's don't. Result: chatters spend 80% less time searching, 80% more time selling.

sources.quote: we label everything. categories, scripts, boy, girl, tier list, jerk off instructions, rapport building, every single pricing for the video, and the script order
we build custom hotkeys per creator, per script, and they are specific to the way she speaks, the way she looks
our reply rate went up by like 60% in the first two weeks just from this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no documented vault structure, hotkey system, or script-order protocol internally. This is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap. Palm's AI chatting project should also encode this structure: a well-categorized vault + conditional script logic maps directly to an AI chat decision tree.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team and AI chatting system, design vault structure and hotkeys as launch-day requirements, not later optimizations. For each creator: (1) categorize all vault content at onboarding, (2) price-tag every piece, (3) define a numbered script order with conditional branches, (4) build hotkeys matched to that creator's voice/aesthetic stored in CreatorHero or equivalent. For the AI chat project, the script-order logic and conditional branches are the conversation flow architecture — treat the vault structure doc as the training data schema.

new_findings.title: Diversify traffic across platforms proactively to hedge against IG ban waves

variants.claim: Agencies relying exclusively on Instagram for traffic are highly exposed to ban waves; diversifying to other platforms (non-IG) immediately before or during a ban wave protects revenue and can generate significant incremental net profit within weeks.
variants.what_they_do: When IG bans hit Renato's clients hard, he prescribed a deliberate step-back from Instagram and shifted effort to unspecified 'other platforms' (intentionally not named — paid coaching content). Client Ricardo grew accounts on new platforms from scratch organically (no paid ads), saw minimal traction in week one, then hit 20K extra net profit in the following 10 days using 3–4 accounts totaling ~33K followers. Renato describes the method as 'full AI marketing' targeting USA audiences. The tactic is explicitly framed as platform-risk mitigation, not just growth.

sources.quote: we took a step back from Instagram as Instagram is banning way too many accounts right now
half of his Instagram accounts got banned. Half of your Instagram accounts get banned, you cut your pretty much revenue in half.
he got maybe 100, 200, 300 followers, not too much, but then in the remaining 10 days, when the accounts got a bit of traction, he's right now pretty much made 20K extra

palm_comparison.vs_us: §5 GAP — Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented. Palm uses IG and TikTok (via Publer/Telegram) but has no written multi-platform diversification policy or contingency plan for IG ban waves. §4 notes that real-creator warmup/cadence is not formalized. The IG ban risk is live and present for Palm's real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately document a platform-diversification contingency SOP: if any real creator loses >2 IG accounts in a 30-day window, trigger migration of effort to at least one backup platform. Identify which platforms (Reddit, TikTok, Threads, X, Snapchat) Palm can activate quickly given existing infrastructure. Add a 'backup platform' field to the Creators table in Airtable and assign one per active creator now, before a ban event forces a rushed response.

new_findings.title: Choose horizontal (more models) vs vertical (deeper per model) scaling based on profit structure

variants.claim: Agencies should consciously choose between scaling horizontally (more creators, same system) or vertically (fewer creators, maximize each) rather than defaulting to signing as many models as possible, because more models often means more headache without proportionally more profit.
variants.what_they_do: Renato lays out two explicit scaling modes: (1) Horizontal — sign 10+ models, apply the same playbook to each, multiply net profit proportionally; (2) Vertical — take 1–4 models and push each to maximum revenue. He warns that multi-model agencies frequently have low per-person net profit when the team is split 3–4 ways. He recommends operators choose a mode deliberately, then structure hiring, content systems, and deal types accordingly. His own preference trends toward fewer models with salary deals and owned traffic.

sources.quote: you can scale horizontally. So, you can have He has four models. You can sign 10 more models, do the same for them, and you'll be happy.
Most people that have many models, that is just a headache. They're gambling, and their agencies rarely make that much profit.
you can make 500k in gross revenue, but how much is each individual person taking home? 30k? 40k? Well, then it's better to be Alexander.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline for Palm's scaling direction (§10 GAP — no org chart or scaling plan). Palm currently has a small roster of real creators managed by a lean team (Josh, Evan, Amin, editors, external chatters). No documented decision on whether Palm is scaling horizontally or vertically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit scaling thesis — horizontal (more creators through a systemized pipeline) or vertical (deeper value per creator). Given Palm's data-driven content engine (§1) and AI-persona scaling lever (§0/§3), a hybrid thesis may make sense: vertical for real creators (fewer, higher-quality) and horizontal for AI personas. Write this into the agency strategy doc and use it to evaluate every new creator signing.

new_findings.title: Weekly chatter self-review prep with 'bring your worst chats or I'll find them' rule

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to pre-prepare their own good/bad chat examples before a weekly standup forces self-accountability, reduces manager review time by ~20 hrs/week, and improves chatter quality proactively.
variants.what_they_do: Before each weekly standup call, every chatter (or supervisor representing the chatter team) must prepare: all problems/questions from the week, five chats where they performed well, and five chats where they performed poorly. The explicit rule: 'If I find bad chats you didn't show me, I'll be mad — better bring them now while I'm in a good mood.' This flips the review burden to the chatter. The agency owner then only needs ~5 minutes to scan before the call. At scale, one supervisor handles all chatters and consolidates the prep; the owner only runs one standup with the supervisor. The agency estimated this system recovers ~20 hours/week of manager time.

sources.quote: prepare all the problems you have, the questions that you want to answer, five chats where you think it went amazing, and five chats where you think you did shit
if I find chats that are bad and you didn't show them to me in a call, I'm going to be mad
I think it was like 20 hours per week just into reviewing everything

palm_comparison.vs_us: DM monetization is outsourced (§8). No chatter review cadence exists internally. This is directly relevant for the in-house chat team roadmap and should also be proposed to the current external chat team as a reporting structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a weekly standup template (problems, 5 good chats, 5 bad chats) and propose it to the external chat team now as a light-touch governance layer. When building the in-house team, make this a non-negotiable onboarding SOP item from day one. Build a Slack/Notion form for chatters to submit their prep asynchronously before the call.

new_findings.title: Train chatters on a fixed funnel: questions, imposition, pull-push, guilt trips

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

sources.quote: that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting with no internal SOP/scripts/funnel (§8 OUTSOURCED); this is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house and AI chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this questions→imposition→pull-push→script→guilt-trip framework as the backbone SOP for Palm's future in-house chat team and as AI-chat training material.

new_findings.title: A defined niche, not looks or tech, separates earners

variants.claim: The biggest driver of five-figure outcomes is a hyper-specific model niche selling a defined fantasy and connection, not production quality or attractiveness.
variants.what_they_do: Reject 'basic blonde' generic models; build niche verticals (nurse, air-hostess, alt/gamer, ethnicity, trans) so 'fans feel she's for them', raising perceived value and scarcity; enforce that the niche/branding stays consistent across bio, highlights, reels, names and the Fanvue vault.

sources.quote: It's the niche
you're selling connection and fantasy

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1 MATURE) decides what to film from viral data but is not framed around per-creator niche/fantasy positioning; positioning is implicit, not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an explicit niche/positioning step to the inspo pipeline for real creators and enforce cross-platform branding consistency; high-value overlap with current work.

new_findings.title: Price identical content by traffic source and proven spend

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission/PPV model (§7 THIN — 'the #1 creator concern...our position isn't written down').
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a source- and spend-based PPV pricing matrix for real creators now and bake it into chatter guidance; a direct fill for the §7 pricing gap.

new_findings.title: Master each channel yourself, then delegate to cheap geo-matched VAs

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

sources.quote: easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is partly delegated (Amin manual posting via Telegram §5; editors §1) but staffing/hiring criteria, scorecards and SOP standards are a gap (§10 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a VA hiring + SOP + geo-matching playbook for posting/social roles to fill the §10 staffing gap; applies to current real-creator posting ops.

new_findings.title: AI-image watermarks (SynthID) are the new ban trigger; switch generators

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI program is EU AI Act compliant with required AI label + watermark (§3), the opposite stance; for real creators Palm has no documented platform-risk/ban-avoidance policy (§4/§5 gaps).
palm_comparison.recommendation: AI line: note label/watermark compliance is in direct tension with the ban-evasion tactics gurus use, so plan for platform-compliant distribution. Real creators: adopt the human-looking-bio + safe link-provider hygiene now.

new_findings.title: Coach creators on 4 non-negotiable quality standards: lighting, audio, background, and variety

variants.claim: Agencies should proactively coach creators on production quality during onboarding rather than waiting for bad content to arrive and damage relationships.
variants.what_they_do: They explicitly advise creators on: (1) lighting — minimum ring light or studio light for long-form video, softer/natural for sexting sets to avoid an overly-produced look; (2) audio — no background noise; (3) background — clean, uncluttered; (4) variety — multiple outfits, angles, lingerie styles, and outdoor locations. This is delivered as part of the onboarding process, not as reactive feedback.

sources.quote: lighting is probably the number one most important thing when it comes to taking high quality content make sure that you're using at least a ring light
make sure that the background of the videos and the pictures and everything doesn't have a bunch like a mess or Garbage

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) evaluates *incoming* inspo reels for viral score but there is no documented creator-facing quality brief or production standards SOP. Quality feedback is presumably reactive via the revision workflow (Tasks table).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Content Quality Standards' PDF/Notion doc covering the four pillars above. Send it during onboarding and reference it in weekly content briefs. Add a quality-gate field to the Assets Airtable table so editors can flag substandard submissions for coaching rather than silently fixing or discarding.

new_findings.title: Use a tiered 'script' — escalating PPV ladder from $15 to $200+ per session

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or chat script structure (§7 GAP, §8 OUTSOURCED). No internal baseline exists for escalation sequencing. This is high-value build-toward intel for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a canonical escalation script template for chatters: define 5–6 tiers with explicit price points, content-reveal thresholds, and transition copy at each step. Treat it as a reusable asset deployed across all creators. Price anchoring should start low ($15) to minimize entry friction. Prioritize this for the AI chatting project where scripts can be fully automated.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Anchor the PPV ladder at $15 entry point to minimize first-purchase friction

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP — identified as #1 creator concern). This is the clearest external data point available for building Palm's first PPV pricing SOP. The doubling structure is a concrete starting model.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this ladder structure as the baseline for Palm's PPV pricing SOP: 6 tiers starting at $15, roughly doubling each step. Document it formally in §7 pricing docs. Test against the external chat team's current pricing to see if a ladder approach could increase per-fan revenue. This also gives Palm a concrete pricing framework to share with creators during onboarding (addressing the #1 creator concern).

new_findings.topic: Cold outreach via Instagram personal brand
new_findings.title: Build a professional IG persona to run cold outreach to creators

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

sources.quote: you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6) but no documented IG persona setup, photo standard, or structured funnel from IG profile → DM → FaceTime → sign. The free-trial-then-sign tactic is not in Palm's playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a Palm acquisition IG profile standard: photo style, bio copy, niche positioning. Add a free-trial offer as a low-friction entry for creators already earning but skeptical. Build a short outreach sequence: IG DM → value hook → FaceTime close → contract. Cheap to implement, directly addresses §6 gap.

new_findings.title: Build a linear pre-made script ladder: message → soft image → message → explicit PPV

variants.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes a reusable script sequence: opener message → follow-up message → half-nude image send → bridging message → full explicit PPV offer → continuation. Recommends doing 6–7 hours of chatting personally first to learn the craft before hiring chatters. Treats this as the primary revenue driver (80–90% of OF revenue).

sources.quote: you want to make one message, then another, then maybe send her a half nude image, then another message, then a full nude, then blah blah blah
80 to 90% is made through chatting — the models, they're busy creating content, so they don't actually have time to do all the chatting

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team today (§8). No internal script, ladder structure, or SOP exists. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team (~1yr out) and the active AI chatting project. Frame as build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin drafting Palm's first pre-made script ladder now as a build-toward asset: (1) map a 5–7 step message sequence per creator persona, (2) define PPV price points per step (ties to §7 pricing gap), (3) document it in Airtable as a 'Chat Scripts' table linked to each creator. This becomes the training baseline when in-house chatting launches.

new_findings.title: Inject scripted rapport breaks and push-pull frames throughout chat to create chase dynamic

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

sources.quote: a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. This is a differentiating technique that separates high-performing chatters from low ones and is absent from most agency training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include rapport breaks and push-pull scripting as a dedicated module in Palm's in-house chatter training. Provide 10–15 templated examples per technique that chatters memorize, then practice in role-play before going live. Monitor for correct usage in chat reviews — a chatter who never uses these is likely glazing, which suppresses sales.

new_findings.topic: PPV value ladder and first-sale anchoring
new_findings.title: Value ladder starting at sub-$5 to condition purchasing habit before escalating price

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

sources.quote: the first sale should be under $5 should be $5 or less why because everyone can afford $5
seven separate sales so it could be one you know number one could be one pick number two could be a pick in a video

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented PPV pricing model at Palm (§7 GAP). The '7 sales per set' structure and sub-$5 entry point are concrete, adoptable mechanics. Palm's outsourced chat team may have their own approach, but it is undocumented internally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this ladder as Palm's default PPV pricing framework when building the in-house chat team. Codify: (1) first PPV always ≤$5, (2) 7 sales-attempt structure per set, (3) incremental price increases per sale, (4) subs who decline the entry price are de-prioritized. Feed this into the Fan Tracker (§9) so chatters can see where each fan sits on the ladder and what the next logical price point is.

new_findings.topic: Reddit and X as supplementary traffic channels
new_findings.title: Contract out Reddit and X content distribution as a low-overhead traffic stream

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

sources.quote: I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit or X traffic strategy (§5 GAP). This is a pure learn-from-others gap that applies to real creators and is relatively easy to test at low cost.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Source a Reddit/X contractor via OFM Telegram groups. Start with a 30-day paid pilot (~$500–$1K) for one creator, using UTM-tagged links or OF referral tracking to attribute revenue. If 5x+ ROI is confirmed, formalize as a recurring line item per creator.

new_findings.topic: Pre-launch network building before signing creators
new_findings.title: Build distribution network of 10–20 partner creators before signing your first creator in a niche

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

sources.quote: before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led and reactive (§6 THIN). There is no documented pre-signing network-building step or launch-day traffic playbook. Palm typically figures out distribution after a creator is onboarded.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any new niche Palm enters (including trans if that becomes a focus), add a pre-launch checklist step: minimum N partner contacts secured in Telegram before creator onboarding begins. Document required contacts and deal commitments as a gate in the creator onboarding flow, making distribution readiness a precondition of launch rather than an afterthought.

new_findings.topic: Organic social as primary agency scaling lever
new_findings.title: Scale organic social ops as the #1 agency growth lever in 2026

variants.claim: The largest OFM agencies earning hundreds of millions annually built their scale on excellent organic social content years ago, and the rest of the industry must catch up now.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant frames organic social content production — not chatting infrastructure or paid acquisition — as the defining competency separating top-tier agencies from the rest, calling for agencies to laser-focus on producing excellent creative and scaling their social media operations.

sources.quote: 2026 is the year you have to get good at organic social. It is time to laser focus on producing excellent creative and scaling your social media ops
the platforms merely ask for one thing in return. Just stop making dog shit. Create content that people actually want to watch

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already has a mature, data-driven organic social pipeline (§1) built around viral inspo scraping and an 8-step production workflow. Palm is ahead of most competitors here. However, Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP), which limits the ability to truly 'scale' social ops systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm should codify the real-creator posting cadence (frequency, platform-specific timing) to complete the §1 system. Additionally, document Palm's organic social capability as a formal competitive differentiator for use in creator acquisition pitches (§6). Consider producing case-study evidence of social growth results to use in signing conversations.

new_findings.title: Label every asset with platform-usage codes to avoid reuse conflicts

variants.claim: Tagging each image file with short codes (e.g. RS, RN, T, PPV) for every platform it has appeared on prevents accidental reuse and lets chatters/managers instantly see which assets are still available for a given platform.
variants.what_they_do: Each uploaded image is numbered sequentially and given a suffix string of platform codes: RS = Reddit selfie subreddit, RN = Reddit niche/kink subreddit, T = Tinder/Bumble, PPV = uploaded to OF vault. A legend document defines all codes. When selecting content for a new post the manager scans the suffix — an image showing only 'T' has never been used on Reddit and is free to post there. Once used, the new code is appended. Content is pre-sorted into category folders: Kinks, Lingerie, Nude/NSFW, Reddit-specific, Social Media (SFW).

sources.quote: you number them all one two whatever and then there's a letter for each instance it's been used on that platform
rsrnt which means it's been used on the Reddit selfie, the Reddit Niche and dating apps which pretty much means you can't really use that image again

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1, §2) tracks Assets and Tasks in Airtable and manages editing/approval/scheduling, but there is no documented system for tracking which individual asset has been deployed to which external platform (IG, Reddit, dating apps, OF feed vs vault). The distinction between OF vault/PPV assets and social assets exists structurally but cross-platform deduplication is not codified (§2 is THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Platform Usage' multi-select or tag field to the Airtable Assets table (or a linked 'Deployments' table) that logs each platform an asset has been posted to. This prevents duplicate posts across Reddit, dating apps, IG, and OF, and gives chatters a clear view of what PPV content is available and unused. A simple legend doc should accompany the field definitions.

new_findings.title: Replicate local folder structure 1-to-1 on Google Drive as business-continuity backup

variants.claim: If the agency's local machine were destroyed, the business should still be able to operate — so every folder and file that is critical to operations is mirrored on Google Drive in the exact same structure.
variants.what_they_do: The agency maintains a Google Drive that mirrors the local computer: a top-level 'Raw' folder per creator containing sub-folders for raw content, photo grid, invoices, promotional assets, OF stories, and the services agreement (contract). The Raw folder is shared directly with the model (right-click → share access → pink highlight convention so the manager knows it is externally shared) so models can self-upload content.

sources.quote: if my laptop dropped or fell in a pool or caught on fire or lightning hit it or a car ran it over could the business still operate if the answer is no then I back everything up to the Google Drive
the Royal folder pink that means that this folder has been shared with the model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Airtable + Cloudflare media for asset storage (§1) and Dropbox for invoice delivery (§7). A formal business-continuity / disaster-recovery policy for media assets is not documented. The model self-upload workflow exists (creators upload to become Assets) but the specific shared-folder colour-coding convention and the 'can the business function without this laptop' checklist mindset are not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a formal backup policy: define which data stores are business-critical (Airtable, Cloudflare media, contract PDFs, invoices) and ensure at least one redundant copy exists outside the primary system. For creator onboarding, consider a shared Google Drive or Cloudflare folder (colour/tag-coded as 'creator-accessible') as the upload landing zone, mirroring the model-upload convenience described here.

new_findings.topic: Reddit minimum viable setup for new agencies
new_findings.title: Start Reddit with one VA, one phone, one account per creator — no scheduling tools, no black hat

variants.claim: Any agency new to Reddit should start with the simplest possible setup — a dedicated VA with a phone-based account posting organically to well-matched subreddits — before investing in black-hat infrastructure, because even this baseline can generate 10+ paid subs/day.
variants.what_they_do: Expert's explicit minimum recommendation regardless of agency size: assign one VA per creator for Reddit, have them use a physical phone (not desktop — Reddit deprioritises desktop activity), post organically every day or every other day to keep the account warm, no scheduling software, no automation, no upvote manipulation. Find the right subreddits through manual trial first. This single-account approach can yield 10+ paid subs/day on a well-matched creator with hundreds of viable organic NSFW subreddits available.

sources.quote: get a dedicated VA. Not no scheduling, no post scheduling, nothing. If you're new to Reddit, get a dedicated VA… just posts every day in or every other day to keep the account warm
one account could definitely get you, let's say, anywhere upwards from 10 paid subs per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently uses Publer for scheduling (§5) and Telegram routing to Amin for manual posting. The phone-native, no-scheduler Reddit requirement is architecturally different. Palm has no VA role defined for Reddit posting (§10 GAP — org structure thin). This is the lowest-risk entry point for Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm pilots Reddit for even one creator, assign a VA (or redirect Amin) to phone-based manual posting. Do not route Reddit through Publer — Reddit flags scheduler signatures. Allocate 30 days of organic posting to 20–50 well-matched NSFW subreddits before evaluating whether black-hat investment is warranted. Track subs attributed to Reddit via UTM or unique landing page.

new_findings.topic: Threads as underrated early-mover traffic source
new_findings.title: Threads new-account first-post boost: exploit algorithmic push for fast early traffic

variants.claim: Threads algorithmically amplifies brand-new accounts' first posts, making it faster to gain initial traction than Twitter/X, where momentum takes much longer to build.
variants.what_they_do: Create a new Threads account, post immediately — the first post gets organic reach from Threads' push. Content is simple engaging text (e.g. 'somebody FaceTime me' style posts targeting lonely men). Bio links to Telegram rather than directly to Fanvue, then Telegram DMs are used to warm and convert. Positioned as a 'hidden gem' due to platform newness.

sources.quote: Threads is really underrated. It can bring in a lot of traffic especially right now because it's more of a newer platform
when you make a brand new Threads account or get a Threads account right off the bat, your first post is going to get views

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting/distribution strategy is not documented for Threads (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm uses Publer for IG/TikTok scheduling but Threads is not mentioned. No Telegram-as-intermediate-funnel step is documented at Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Test Threads for AI personas immediately while the new-account boost window exists. Start with simple text-based engagement posts. For the AI persona line, use the Telegram intermediate step (persona Telegram → Fanvue) since it adds a warm pre-sell layer before the paid platform. Evaluate adding Threads to Publer scheduling (Phase 3 — §5).

new_findings.title: Automate X/Twitter R4R via Xbox bot + antidetect browser for passive low-effort traffic

variants.claim: Twitter R4R (retweet-for-retweet) automated via the Xbox bot is the easiest traffic source for beginners and runs 24/7 with minimal setup, though it delivers lower-quality fans than Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Create X account, warm for 24 hours, purchase X Premium, join retweet groups, set up antidetect browser, install Xbox bot. Make one post, pin it, start the bot — it handles R4R continuously. The agency positions this as a side channel, not primary, due to lower subscriber quality.

sources.quote: Twitter, all we do is just retweet for retweet using Xbox… the bot does the rest for you. Does route 24/7
Twitter isn't the highest quality, but it is the easiest. So it's definitely good to do on the side

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal Twitter/X traffic strategy documented at Palm (§5 🔲 GAP). This is a zero-marginal-cost automation layer Palm could layer on top of existing content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Test X R4R as a low-priority side channel for 2–3 real creators: one account each, Xbox bot + antidetect browser, run for 30 days and measure OF click-throughs via unique tracking links. If CTR justifies the account management overhead, systematize. Keep expectation low — use as supplementary, not primary.

new_findings.topic: IG account warmup — buying aged accounts with short warmup
new_findings.title: Buy aged IG accounts and run only 3–5 day warmup by engaging OF creator content

variants.claim: Bought aged IG accounts need only a 3–5 day warmup (engaging OF creator posts, scrolling) before going live — longer warmups recommended by others are unnecessary in practice.
variants.what_they_do: Purchase aged IG accounts to skip karma/history building. Warmup = 3–5 days of scrolling and engaging with OF creator content only. Then set up bio with link.me or Linktree, profile photo (face + slight chest), pin three posts showing key body parts relevant to niche. Post 2 Reels/day. For account isolation: UK/US/AU/CA creators can use home Wi-Fi unless ban rate exceeds ~10 accounts; otherwise use VPN/proxy.

sources.quote: we buy our accounts aged and then we do a warm up for around like… 3 to 5 day warm up of just interacting OF creators engaging with the posts scrolling and that's it
IG is not as strict as people think it is… if you're in the UK, US, Australia, Canada… then you can use mobile Wi-Fi

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-account warmup is mature and fully specified at 90 days (§4 MATURE for AI). Real-creator IG warmup is flagged as a 🔲 GAP (§4 THIN for real). This competitor uses a much faster, buy-then-brief-warmup approach for real creators specifically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For real-creator IG scaling, trial the buy-aged-account + 3–5 day warmup approach on 2–3 accounts. Compare survival rate and early engagement vs. any accounts Palm currently creates from scratch. If aged accounts perform comparably, formalize as the real-creator IG onboarding SOP — it compresses time-to-post dramatically.

new_findings.topic: Chatting audit: script-persona correlation + right-sizing chatter count
new_findings.title: Audit chatter scripts for persona alignment and cut excess chatters to unlock revenue

variants.claim: The single highest-impact revenue intervention at $50K+/mo is an audit of chatting: verify mass messages and scripts match the creator's personality sheet, and fire chatters whose pages are underutilized — this caused a same-day revenue spike.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin and Jason logged into Barry's Inflow (chatting platform) as admins and ran a three-part audit: (1) identified what was wrong with chatting mechanics; (2) verified all mass messages and automation scripts correlated with each creator's personality sheet; (3) identified and fired unnecessary chatters — reducing from 6 to 4 — freeing the remaining chatters to work larger, more active pages. Revenue spiked the same day.

sources.quote: we went through all your mass messages we first identified what was wrong with your chatting and we looked at the correlation between all your personality sheets of your clients with the chatting
firing unnecessary chatters because you had like six chatters at the time when you really only needed four

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team (§8). There is no documented internal SOP for auditing chatting quality, script-persona correlation, or chatter headcount optimization. This is high-value build-toward intelligence for Palm's roadmap (in-house chat team + AI chatting projects).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's in-house chat team roadmap (§8): build a chatter audit protocol as a core management tool — (1) persona sheet ↔ script correlation check, (2) mass message / automation review, (3) chatter-to-page load analysis with a defined optimal ratio. Even before building in-house, Palm should request this audit cadence from the current external team. Capture Inflow as a platform to evaluate.

new_findings.topic: High-volume posting scale strategy — trial reel method
new_findings.title: 'Trial reel strategy': scale winning format to 20 reels/day in a defined posting order

variants.claim: Once a winning reel format is identified in the 10-day test, scaling to 20 trial reels per day in a specific sequence dramatically accelerates follower and subscriber growth beyond what single-daily posting can achieve.
variants.what_they_do: After the 10-day test identifies the top-performing content format, the operator switches to posting 20 reels per day using Instagram's 'trial reel' feature (which shows reels to non-followers first), in a 'specific order' the creator says he will cover in a follow-up video. This is presented as the primary scale lever once product-market fit on content is confirmed, and is credited with averaging 50+ subscribers/day.

sources.quote: you're going to do the trial reel strategy which is posting 20 trial reels a day in a specific order
I average more than 50 subscribers a day with my Instagram strategies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting infrastructure (§5) currently routes through Publer and manual Telegram/Amin posting with a cadence of roughly 2 tasks/day per creator (editor queue). A 20-reels/day volume is an entirely different operational scale — Palm has no documented framework for this. The trial reel feature (IG shows to non-followers before publishing to feed) is not referenced in Palm's pipeline documentation.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Investigate Instagram's native 'trial reel' feature and assess whether it applies to real creator accounts (not just AI). If applicable, prototype it with one creator at the 10-day test conclusion. For AI personas (§3/§4), plan the infrastructure for high-volume scheduling (Publer bulk upload, content batching) as a Phase 2 of the AI warmup cadence — the current 90-day warmup plan (§4) has no provision for scaling to 20 posts/day once the account matures. Capture full 'trial reel ordering' mechanic when the follow-up video is published.

new_findings.title: Standardize all scripts and objection-handling so mid-conversation shift handoffs are seamless

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

sources.quote: standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script or handoff SOP. This is a critical build-toward finding: the revenue leak at shift change is quantified ($1–2K/day at scale) and the fix is documentation, not hiring.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing the in-house chat system, treat shift-handoff continuity as a first-class requirement. Build a conversation-state handoff field (current PPV sequence step, last offer made, fan emotional state, open objections) into whatever CRM or chat tool is used. Standardize scripts before hiring chatters — not after.

new_findings.topic: Fan segmentation — low vs high spenders
new_findings.title: Segment fans by max spend ($50 vs $120+) and use different opening tactics per tier

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

sources.quote: if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module (§9) that tracks spend data, but no documented segmented selling playbook or per-tier opener scripts. The data infrastructure exists; the chatter execution layer does not.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Map these two tiers onto Palm's existing Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data. For the in-house chat team, write two distinct opener script banks keyed to max-spend threshold. The 'question game' opener is particularly low-friction and could be A/B tested quickly once chatting is brought in-house. The lapsed-whale personalized PPV ($150–200, notes-referenced caption) is a high-ROI re-engagement tactic worth standardizing.

new_findings.title: PPV Anchors: Plant a story or fantasy 6–24 hours before sending the PPV to manufacture perceived relevance

variants.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

sources.quote: we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal SOP for pre-seeding PPV context exists (§8 outsourced, §2 thin on PPV vault strategy). Palm does have a content posting pipeline (§1) and scheduling infrastructure (§5), which could be used to coordinate feed-level anchors with chatter follow-up timing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 'PPV anchor' protocol into the future in-house chat SOP: define anchor types (DM-personal vs feed-broadcast), specify the delay window (6–24 hrs), and create a coordination mechanism between the posting schedule (Publer/Telegram) and the chat team so chatters know when an anchor post goes live and can follow up. This is a high-leverage tactic because it uses existing content infrastructure.

new_findings.topic: Indirect sexting as PPV pitch mechanism
new_findings.title: Pitch PPVs via 'indirect sexting' — escalate a fantasy tangent, then link the PPV as the physical manifestation

variants.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatting scripts or sexting frameworks exist at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is a build-toward tactic for the in-house chat team and AI chatting project. The AI chatting project in particular would need this fantasy-ladder logic codified as a sequencing rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the in-house chat roadmap, build a 'fantasy library' per creator (ranked low-to-high explicitness) that chatters and eventually AI chatters draw from sequentially per subscriber. Document the indirect sexting structure as a script template: embarrassed open → full fantasy build → PPV link with timestamped personal connection. This is especially relevant for the AI chatting project where consistent, structured dialogue trees are required.

new_findings.topic: Outreach message framing — authority + personalization, no direct pitch
new_findings.title: Open with a conversion-problem hook; never pitch the agency in the first DM

variants.claim: The first DM should establish authority by identifying a specific problem in the creator's setup rather than pitching the agency, and should funnel toward a no-pressure discovery call rather than explaining the revenue split.
variants.what_they_do: Template opener: 'Hey [Name], I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions.' Then a brief authority statement. Never mention the split in DMs — save that for the call. On the call: ask current earnings, show what's possible, then close on next steps and send a contract via Dropbox.

sources.quote: Hey Name, I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions
Don't explain the split on the DMs. Obviously, explain the split on the actual call itself

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message template or sales-call funnel (§6 GAP). The split is set per-creator at onboarding but no pre-call qualifying script exists (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Draft a 2–3 message DM sequence template: (1) problem-hook opener with authority, (2) 24-hr follow-up voice note, (3) call-to-action to book a 10–15 min discovery call. Create a call script with qualifying questions (current earnings, goals) before revealing the split. Store both in the acquisition runbook.

new_findings.topic: Creator quality bar — A-tier targeting vs. settling
new_findings.title: Hold out for A-tier creators; 3 months with a weak creator costs more than 3 months outreaching

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

sources.quote: if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator qualification criteria or scoring system (§6 GAP). Onboarding form captures name, email, commission %, state — but no quality or potential assessment.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a written creator qualification rubric (follower range, content consistency, responsiveness, existing OF presence, appearance tier) to be applied before any sales call is booked. Add a qualification score field to the Creators Airtable table so onboarding decisions are data-driven, not intuition-based.

new_findings.title: Pre-shoot 'verification pictures' (peace sign, spoon, etc.) to neutralize fan skepticism during sessions

variants.claim: Having a library of pre-shot 'random gesture' verification photos allows chatters to instantly defuse fans who doubt the content is being filmed live for them.
variants.what_they_do: Before any DM session, the model shoots a bank of verification-style photos — holding a peace sign, thumbs up, spoon on head, etc. When a fan challenges authenticity ('I don't believe you're doing this live'), the chatter sends one of these photos on demand. Because the images look spontaneous and fan-requestable, they eliminate doubt without the model needing to actually film anything new.

sources.quote: you need verification pictures. So to any pictures, this is just basically like holding a peace sign, thumbs up, maybe a spoon on your head
you can just send a picture like this or he will even maybe request like hold a piece and hold a spoon on your head and you have them ready to eliminate that thought

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline for this. Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented asset library concept for DM sessions. This is a build-toward gap for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'verification shot bank' as a mandatory deliverable in the OF vault/content brief for any creator whose DM sessions will use a scripted approach. Define minimum: 5–10 varied gesture photos, shot in the same session as the script content (consistent lighting/wardrobe). Document this in the future in-house chat SOP.

new_findings.topic: DM session structure and rapport phase
new_findings.title: Open every DM session with 5–20 message rapport phase before any sexual pivot

variants.claim: Every DM session — whether with a new or returning subscriber — should open with a non-sexual rapport phase to qualify the fan and create emotional investment before monetization begins.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers: 5–20 messages gathering personal info (job, hobbies) with light flirting, zero sexual content. Returning subscribers: brief small talk, ask about their day, deploy inside jokes if available. Only after this rapport block does the chatter pivot toward sexting. The report section is explicitly never scripted — it must feel genuine — but everything after it is.

sources.quote: First thing you want to do when a fan comes to the page is build report. This is five to 20 messages depending on the fan and the communication
The report part is never scripted because it has to feel genuine, but everything after that is scripted

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal rapport SOP exists. This is a high-signal build-toward finding. The distinction between scripted vs. unscripted phases is particularly useful for designing chatter training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, encode a mandatory unscripted rapport phase at session open. Define minimum message count by subscriber type (new: ~10 msgs; returning: ~5 msgs). Train chatters that this phase is intentionally freeform — the goal is genuine connection data that feeds downstream personalization. Document inside-joke capture as a CRM field (Palm already has Fan Tracker §9 — add an 'inside jokes / personal details' field per fan).

new_findings.topic: PPV ladder and first-PPV price anchoring
new_findings.title: Price first PPV at $15–$25 and use a 'Yes Train' commitment loop to pre-sell before sending

variants.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.

sources.quote: the yes train is basically getting a confirmation, getting a yes from him so that when later you send the PPV, he feels obliged to buy it because he already said yes
first PPV you will price $15 to $25 this depends on your model where you market her and how you market her

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or ladder (§7 GAP — noted as '#1 creator concern'). This is a concrete, tested pricing structure from a $100k/month operation. The 'yes train' pre-sell mechanic is also absent from any internal SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this as a starting-point PPV ladder for the in-house chat team: first PPV $15–$25 (partial nudity), second PPV (full nudity) $20–$35, third+ (solo play) $25–$50+. Adjust per creator tier. Document the 'yes train' as a mandatory pre-PPV step in the chat SOP. Also use this ladder to inform the pricing section (§7) — this is the most concrete competitor data Palm has on PPV structure.

new_findings.title: Replace generic greetings with open-ended, personalized openers

variants.claim: Using open-ended, flirty questions instead of generic greetings like 'hey' or 'how are you' dramatically increases subscriber engagement.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to never open with 'hello' or 'how are you'; instead they ask about the fan's day in a flirty, playful way, reference previous conversations ('you made me blush'), and use the subscriber's name periodically to create intimacy. Example opener: 'Hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush.'

sources.quote: use open-ended questions instead of just typing hello or greetings ask engaging questions avoid asking how or you because it is so boring
instead of copy pasting a generic hey try messaging hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced to an external chat team (§8); no internal SOP or scripted opener standards exist. This is directly relevant build-toward material for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat SOP, codify a banned-phrases list ('hey', 'how are you', 'okay') and a required-opener format: fan name + callback to prior interaction + flirty open-ended question. Embed this in chatter onboarding scripts.

new_findings.title: Send delayed, personalized one-liners as first message — never paragraphs or PPVs

variants.claim: A short, delayed welcome message (3 min after sub) that references the fan's profile bio/name outperforms generic paragraphs because it signals authenticity and avoids setting a low price anchor.
variants.what_they_do: Use Creator Hero's delayed-message feature to send a simple 'Hi' or name-based joke 3 minutes after subscription. If no reply, follow up with a profile-specific observation (name joke, bio reference) to signal a real person is behind the account. Never send a PPV or long paragraph as the first message.

sources.quote: It's better to just say, 'Hi, hello, what's up?' than to write him a whole paragraph as a welcoming message
you can look at his profile, his bio, his name, and you can just say something about his name, make a joke

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP or scripts. There is no documented welcome-message protocol. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, codify: (1) use delayed send ~3 min post-sub, (2) open with ≤1 short line referencing fan's name/bio, (3) do not price-anchor or send PPV until rapport is established. Document this as Step 1 of the chatter onboarding script.

new_findings.title: Gauge spending power through rapport questions before setting any PPV price

variants.claim: Talking to a fan before quoting any price lets chatters learn his job/lifestyle and set a higher first PPV price ($25 vs $10) because no anchor was established early.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters ask open questions about job and hobbies — not from a script, but organically — to extract spending-power signals. Light flirting and jokes are used; sexual escalation is avoided until the fan initiates. The chatter never names a price before this rapport window closes.

sources.quote: we do use scripts inside our agency, but we never have the rapport part scripted as it's really important to let the conversation run smoothly
you're also going to get a glimpse of his spending power. So for how much you can send him PPVs

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm's whale-hunting module (§9) collects fan spend data post-hoc but there is no documented pre-sale rapport protocol that informs pricing in real time.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the in-house chat SOP, add a mandatory rapport phase (est. 5–10 messages) before any PPV is sent. Train chatters to log job/lifestyle signals in fan notes and use them to bracket PPV price — e.g., blue-collar → $15–25, professional → $30–60. Tie this to the Fan Tracker CRM (§9).

new_findings.title: Run a repeating descriptive-text + free-teaser + PPV loop until fan 'taps out' — then stop selling

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

sources.quote: descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). This is the core revenue engine loop that Palm's outsourced team presumably runs but Palm has no documented version of.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document this loop as the core 'session playbook' in the in-house chatter training. Define a session KPI (revenue per session, PPVs per session) and track per chatter. Pre-record a library of appreciation/wind-down clips for each creator so chatters can deploy them instantly at session end.

new_findings.title: Tag fans with emoji labels in their OF display name for instant at-a-glance segmentation

variants.claim: Adding emoji tags (whale emoji, feet emoji, etc.) directly to a fan's OnlyFans display name lets chatters instantly identify spend tier and kink preference without opening notes, critical when managing high inbox volume.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters edit the fan's name field in the OF inbox to prepend relevant emojis: 🐳 for whale, specific kink emojis (e.g., foot emoji for feet fetish). This means the chatter sees the tag the moment the chat appears in the list and can tailor the opening message without reading back through history.

sources.quote: you can put emojis in their name, right? In their OnlyFans name and you can quickly see what type of subscriber they are
a specific kink they might have if they're into feet. You can put a feet emoji in their name
