palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) stores per-fan data but requires opening a record. This emoji-in-name trick is a zero-friction, platform-native shortcut that works inside OF itself — Palm's CRM layer doesn't replicate this speed.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a Palm emoji taxonomy (tier: 🐳 whale / 💰 spender / ⏳ time-waster; kink tags) and add it to chatter onboarding training. Pair with Fan Tracker: emoji = quick-scan layer in OF; CRM = detailed notes. Low-cost, high-speed UX improvement for any in-house chatter.

new_findings.title: Use 50/50 split-payment to overcome custom content price objections without discounting

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: No pricing or custom-content SOP at Palm (§7 gap, §8 outsourced). The 50/50 mechanic is a concrete objection-handling script Palm can directly adopt for both the in-house chat roadmap and any custom-content pricing guide.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add the 50/50 split-pay offer as a named objection-handling step in the chatter SOP, positioned before any price reduction. Also define a custom content pricing floor (e.g., 3–5× standard PPV price) so chatters know what 'premium' means before quoting. Document this in the pricing model (§7 gap).

new_findings.title: Log fan hobbies, fetishes, and job in subscriber notes to personalize DMs

variants.claim: Building rapport with subscribers requires learning what they are genuinely into (fetishes, hobbies, job) and recording those details in the subscriber's notes field to enable personalized follow-up.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters skip surface-level questions (age, location) and instead probe for fetishes, hobbies, and occupation. Every paying subscriber gets a notes entry with those details so any chatter picking up the conversation later has context. Non-spenders are deprioritized — no time is wasted building rapport with subs who have not spent money.

sources.quote: what are they into right maybe they what do they do for work what are their Hobbies obviously if we're talking about only fans specifically what are they into like what are their fetishes
you don't want to be able to report with people who are just going to waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced (§8). Palm does have a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9) that capture per-fan data, but no documented SOP for what chatters should log or how to use notes to personalize conversations. This tactic speaks directly to both the build-toward in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, include a mandatory 'fan profile' step: before sending a PPV or upsell, chatters must log fetish, hobby, and occupation for any subscriber who has spent money. Gate the fan profile requirement at first purchase, not first message, to avoid wasting effort on non-buyers. Align this with the existing Fan Tracker table (§9).

new_findings.topic: Multi-platform traffic strategy and platform volatility
new_findings.title: Diversify traffic sources across TikTok, Instagram Reels, and Reddit bot services because platform effectiveness shifts month to month

variants.claim: No single platform is the permanent best traffic source — TikTok and Instagram Reels alternate in effectiveness over weeks, while Reddit bot services offer more stable, updatable automation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitors which platform is currently converting and shifts effort accordingly (e.g., moved from TikTok to IG Reels during a two-month TikTok slump, then back). Reddit bot/automation services are treated as a stable baseline because they can be updated when Reddit changes its algorithm or removes bots. Maintains awareness of Reddit API/bot policy changes.

sources.quote: there is no best it always varies Tick Tock for the last two weeks it is working but the last two months before that it was very very like it just genuinely it was just bad so Instagram reels were better so it always changes
things that work really really well at least for us that we found are like bot Services they don't really mess up and it's pretty easy to then update these bot Services as well

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is an explicit GAP (§5). Palm posts via Publer and Telegram but has no documented platform-rotation or bot-service layer. Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on ideation but silent on which platform gets what content when.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'traffic pulse' review into Palm's weekly ops cadence: each week, flag which platform (TikTok vs IG Reels vs Reddit) is producing the most OF link clicks per creator. Use this to dynamically weight posting effort. Separately, evaluate Reddit bot/automation services as a low-maintenance evergreen traffic channel and document Palm's position on their use given platform-risk considerations (§platform-risk).

new_findings.title: Main + 2-4 Burner Account Structure to Grow X Presence Fast

variants.claim: Running one premium 'main' account supported by 2–4 high-volume burner accounts is the core engine for rapid X follower growth and OF revenue.
variants.what_they_do: One main account is treated as the premium asset — high quality content only, no full nudity, goal of 50–100K impressions per post. Two to four burner accounts run parallel at higher volume (3–5 posts/day vs 1–3 on main), used for trend testing, engagement farming, and follower accumulation. Once burners hit 1–5K followers, all traffic is funneled to the main via pinned tweets, bio links, and reply CTAs. Main account holds the OF link; burners never monetize directly.

variants.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.

sources.quote: You're going to have two to four accounts in this depending on where you're at. The account one is going to be your main account.
These are your growth engine

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is a documented GAP (§5). Palm has no X/Twitter presence or strategy documented. This is a fully absent channel for Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 1-main + 2-burner X account structure for each real creator. Assign a VA to run burner engagement (spam commenting, trend monitoring, retweet loop) daily. Main account managed by editor/ops. Start with one creator as a test before rolling out.

new_findings.title: Trend-Jack Active X Formats Daily for Guaranteed High-Impression Posts

variants.claim: Hopping on a trending X format with high-quality content almost guarantees 50K–200K+ impressions per post, far outperforming Instagram trend participation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitor X daily for trending formats in the creator's niche. As soon as a format gains traction (e.g., 'Hey Grok, put me in a bikini' AI image trend in Jan 2026), immediately produce a post in that format with high-quality creative and post it. Don't wait for certainty — first 5,000–10,000 participants get disproportionate reach. Historical trend formats used: POV/relatable scenery, 'Which one would you pick' poll + photos, 'Rate my outfit 1–10' mirror pic, AI-generated content prompts, 'This or that' polls. Cadence: check competitor/big accounts daily; if a format is getting traction, copy it fast.

sources.quote: Trendjacking. This is the cheat code. On Twitter, trends are everything. If you hop on a trend correctly and at the right time, you're guaranteed to blow up.
If you're one of the first 5,000 people, 10,000 people to hop on a trend and do it correctly with high quality, guaranteed to blow up.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is already data-driven and scrapes viral Reels from IG inspo accounts — this is exactly the same logic applied to X. Palm's system could theoretically be extended to scrape X trending formats. Currently Palm has no X monitoring in the Apify/inspo pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add X trend monitoring to the content pipeline: include 5–10 X creator accounts in the Apify scrape or add a manual daily X check as a VA task. When a trending format is identified, create an X-specific task card in Airtable so the creator can produce the asset same-day. Mirror Palm's existing inspo-promote-film loop for X.

new_findings.topic: X engagement farming via competitor reply hijacking
new_findings.title: Reply-Hijack Competitor X Posts With Relevant Matching Content to Steal Followers

variants.claim: Replying to competing creators' posts with a relevant matching photo of your model hijacks their engagement and drives profile visits and follows at zero cost.
variants.what_they_do: Identify 10–20 OF creator accounts in the same niche on X. When those accounts post (e.g., bikini beach pic), the burner account replies with a relevant matching photo of the managed creator and a natural caption (e.g., 'What about my bikini at the beach?'). Also retweet competitor posts to appear in front of their audience. Comment on any post going viral in the broader niche to farm free impressions. VA hired to execute this spam engagement loop throughout the day.

sources.quote: Reply to that with relevant things. What about my bikini at the beach? And then attach a photo of you at your model at a beach. What this is going to do is hijack their engagement.
You guys can hire a VA to do this. I have my VAs to do this. Just make sure your spam engagement.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no X presence (§5 GAP). Palm does no competitor engagement farming on any platform currently. The VA execution model aligns with Palm's contractor model (Amin for posting) — this would be a new VA task scope.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When standing up X for a creator, hire or assign a VA specifically for X engagement tasks: daily competitor reply loop, retweet rotation, viral-post comment farming. Define a task card type in Airtable for 'X daily engagement' with a checklist. Budget ~1–2 hrs/day per creator for this role.

new_findings.title: Deploy a chatter supervisor on Day 1 as a proactive asset, not a reactive fix

variants.claim: Adding a dedicated chat supervisor from day one — who reviews 50–60 conversations daily — catches revenue-destroying patterns weeks or months before they'd surface in KPI dashboards, preventing compounding subscriber loss.
variants.what_they_do: On day 1 they assign a 'Hulk supervisor' whose sole job is to pull and deep-analyze 50–60 chats per day. The supervisor checks: script adherence, objection-handling consistency, list management, correct content routing to fans. When within 7 days a pattern of subs dropping after first PPV was spotted, the supervisor flagged it to the backend manager, they reviewed PPV strategy for the week, adjusted price and script. Three chatters on 8-hour shifts give 24/7 coverage; all conversations answered within 70 seconds. Supervisor is added before problems appear — not as damage control after.

sources.quote: we add the supervisor as an asset on day one…the supervisor pulls 50 to 60 chats a day and analyzes deeply
they catch the patterns of the chatters fast…within 7 days of the switch…the supervisor noticed that was like a chunk of subs that were dropping off after the first PPV

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is currently outsourced (§8 — external team). No internal supervisor layer or QA system is documented. This is high-value build-toward intelligence for Palm's roadmap (in-house chat team ~1yr out + AI chatting project). The supervisor-as-day-1-asset framing is a direct structural recommendation for when Palm builds in-house.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, make the supervisor role a launch-day hire (not a later addition). Define the supervisor's daily output: minimum N chats reviewed, a weekly pattern report to ops, and a flag-to-fix SLA (e.g., pattern flagged → backend adjustment within 48 hours). For the AI chatting project, equivalent logic applies: build automated conversation QA from day one rather than retrofitting it. In the interim, ask the current outsourced team to provide weekly pattern reports (drop-off points, reply rate by message type) as a proxy.

new_findings.topic: Fan list segmentation for mass messages
new_findings.title: Segment subscriber list into 5+ tiers; send different mass messages per tier daily

variants.claim: Sending the same mass message to all subscribers burns money; segmenting by purchase behavior and sending tailored tone, CTA, and price to each segment dramatically increases conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Minimum one mass message per day (online or standard). Lists segmented into at minimum: new subscribers, OC (old/churned) list, active selling list (have purchased content), big spenders, no-spenders, time wasters (high engagement, zero purchase). Each segment receives different message tone, CTA, and price point. A 500-dollar spender gets a completely different pitch than a subscriber who joined 8 hours ago. Iteration loop applied to mass messages: if view rate is high but reply rate is low, convert statements to questions ('Would you rather see me in the bikini or out of it?') — reply rate doubles.

sources.quote: We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no documented internal segmentation strategy. Palm's Fan Tracker / whale-hunting system (§9) collects fan spend data, which is the raw material for this segmentation — but there's no documented bridge from that data to mass message targeting. This is an actionable gap even before building in-house chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As an immediate step: share Palm's whale-hunting and Fan Tracker data with the outsourced chat team and request they implement list segmentation (minimum: new, active buyer, big spender, no-spender). As a build-toward step for in-house: define the 5 segment definitions in Airtable against fan spend fields and automate segment tagging so chatters can filter in CreatorHero or equivalent. For the AI chatting roadmap, list segmentation is the primary personalization input — design the data model to support it from day one.

new_findings.topic: Agency commission structure and cost stack
new_findings.title: 35–50% creator split with chatting cost (15–20%) and ad reinvestment (5–10%) baked in

variants.claim: A sustainable agency deal charges creators 35–50% gross, with ~15–20% allocated to chatting, 5–10% reinvested into paid traffic, and the remainder as agency margin.
variants.what_they_do: Agency takes 35–50% of creator revenue depending on workload. Chatting costs the agency 15–20% of that (paid to the chat company, which in his case is his own). Ad spend is a committed 5–10% reinvestment every month — not optional, not discretionary. Higher percentage deals correspond to heavier operational lift. Year-long contracts with a small exit fee, but models are let go without a fight if they want to leave.

sources.quote: we go anywhere from 35 to 50%. Sometimes we'll go a little higher depending on like how much work it is
the chatting component costs anywhere between 15 to 20%... then I'll commit a certain portion of the budget, anywhere between like 5 to 10% depending on the month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has commission % set at onboarding but no documented split rationale or reinvestment commitment (§7 GAP). The cost-stack breakdown (chatting % + ad reinvestment % + margin %) is exactly the modeling Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Palm pricing model that explicitly stacks: (1) chatting cost % (currently outsourced — know your actual rate), (2) committed monthly ad/traffic reinvestment %, (3) operational overhead %, (4) margin floor. Use this to set defensible creator commission tiers rather than ad-hoc per-creator negotiation. Document and show this math to creators during signing — it builds trust and justifies the split.

new_findings.topic: Creator relationship management and transparency as retention
new_findings.title: Radical transparency with creators (admit failures, show money flow) earns long-term loyalty

variants.claim: Being fully transparent with models about where their money goes, admitting when you're failing and why, and framing everything around their long-term income sustainability earns far more loyalty than over-promising.
variants.what_they_do: He tells creators explicitly where agency fees go and how ad spend is allocated. When metrics underperform, he proactively explains why rather than going silent. When a creator wants to leave, he releases them without conflict and often admits his own faults. He reports that 8 out of 10 creators who leave return. He pitches creators with numbers and technical detail — no yachts, no content houses, no collab promises.

sources.quote: being fully transparent is very important to me. If I'm failing at something, I will tell them I'm failing at this thing. Now I'll tell them why I'm failing at it
if you want to leave and go somewhere else, I'm not going to fight you... eight out of 10 times they'll revisit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has weekly invoicing (§7) but no documented creator success cadence, QBR process, or retention strategy (§9 GAP). The specific mechanics here — proactive failure disclosure, no-fight exits, reinvestment transparency — are directly adoptable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a monthly creator check-in cadence for Palm where the manager shares: (1) revenue trend vs. prior period, (2) what's working / what isn't and why, (3) how the agency fee was deployed (ad spend, chatting, etc.). Document the no-fight exit policy explicitly — it costs nothing and statistically earns returns. Add these to Palm's creator onboarding agreement as stated commitments.

new_findings.title: Only sign creators you genuinely believe in and who demonstrate required work ethic

variants.claim: Signing creators indiscriminately — especially by pulling money from your own pocket to invest in unproven talent — leads to repeated disappointment; selective signing with reinvestment funded only from page revenue is more sustainable.
variants.what_they_do: He only signs models he believes he can help and who show the work ethic to succeed. He has stopped funding upfront investment from his own pocket after being burned repeatedly — all reinvestment now comes from the page's own revenue. He describes his current pitch as entirely numbers-based with no lifestyle promises, filtering for creators who respond to data over hype.

sources.quote: I only sign models that I'm interested in working with and that I think I can help
I've gotten away from pulling out of my own pocket and putting money into somebody because I've been disappointed way too many times

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no systematic qualification criteria documented (§6 GAP). There is no written scorecard for what makes a creator signable vs. not, and no documented policy on whether Palm fronts any upfront costs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a Palm creator qualification scorecard covering: existing audience size/engagement, content production consistency, responsiveness/work ethic signals, and revenue potential estimate. Document whether Palm ever funds upfront costs and under what conditions. Use a data-first pitch deck (projected revenue range, what drives each lever) rather than lifestyle/brand promises — this also filters for more serious creators.

new_findings.topic: Chatting ratio as primary agency KPI / revenue leverage framing
new_findings.title: Frame chatting ratio (revenue per subscriber) as the highest-leverage KPI — easier to 3x than traffic, and directly multiplies total revenue

variants.claim: Tripling chatting conversion ratio (e.g. welcome-message reply rate from 2/10 to 6/10) produces the same revenue impact as tripling traffic, but is dramatically easier to achieve — making chatting ratio the single highest-ROI operational metric for an OFM agency.
variants.what_they_do: They explicitly benchmark chatting ratio improvement against traffic growth: getting 3x more subscribers requires algorithm luck, volume, and significant effort; getting 3x the reply rate on a welcome message requires rewriting one message. They use this framing to prioritize chatting optimization above traffic scaling in their operational roadmap and measure success by ratio improvement (replies per welcome message sent, PPV conversion per DM thread, revenue per subscriber) rather than raw subscriber counts. They cite a 5:1 to 10:1 revenue ratio improvement moving an agency from $50K to $100K+ monthly.

sources.quote: tripling your chatting ratio can be to get two out of ten people that reply to a welcome message up to six out of ten
tripling your chatting ratio… you're already making three times the money. It's as easy as that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented KPI framework for chatting (§8, outsourced). The whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) collect data but there's no stated primary metric or ratio that Palm tracks and optimizes. With chatting outsourced, Palm likely has limited visibility into the ratios the external team is producing. This framing is immediately useful for the in-house/AI chat roadmap and for setting SLA expectations with the current external team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and start tracking three chatting ratios now, even with the outsourced team: (1) welcome-message open rate; (2) welcome-message reply rate; (3) PPV conversion rate per active DM thread. Request these metrics from the external chatting team on a weekly basis. Establish baseline numbers before the in-house transition so Palm knows what it's inheriting and what 'good' looks like. When building the in-house team, set ratio improvement targets (not headcount targets) as the primary KPI for the department.

new_findings.topic: Reddit karma farming before promo posting
new_findings.title: Manual 2-week karma farm before any promotional posting

variants.claim: Spending two weeks manually karma-farming — scrolling, joining subreddits, posting a few times, dropping one or two comments per day — prevents Reddit from flagging the account when promo begins.
variants.what_they_do: For the first two weeks after account creation: scroll a few minutes daily, join a variety of subreddits, upload a few normal posts, leave one or two genuine comments per day max. No promo content at all during this window. The goal is account age and karma score so posts are not auto-removed when promo starts.

sources.quote: we karma farm manually for about 2 weeks before we even think about any promo
you shouldn't rush this process. If you try to skip this, Reddit will flag you and the account's dead

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a formalized 90-day warmup cadence for AI social accounts (§4 MATURE for AI) but has no equivalent documented warmup process for Reddit accounts (§5/§6 GAP). The principle is identical — trust-score building before monetization activity — but it has not been applied to Reddit.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a Reddit-specific warmup SOP: 14-day karma farm (a few posts + 1–2 comments/day, no promo) before any OF-linked posting. Consider adding this to the existing warmup infrastructure in Airtable alongside AI account warmup tasks.

new_findings.topic: Reddit subreddit selection — pre-built niche lists
new_findings.title: Use pre-built niche-specific subreddit lists (including 200+ zero-karma subs) to eliminate research time

variants.claim: Maintaining curated subreddit lists segmented by niche — including a dedicated list of 200+ subreddits that accept posts with zero karma — eliminates the time drain of manual subreddit research and reduces the risk of posting in wrong/banned communities.
variants.what_they_do: Franko maintains 10+ lists, one per niche (Latina, milf, teen, boobs, feet, SFW, trans, cosplay, etc.). A separate list of 200+ zero-karma-required subreddits is used specifically for new accounts in the warmup-to-promo transition period. Team members pull the list matching the model's niche and post immediately. A master list of 2,500+ subreddits is available for further expansion.

sources.quote: for new Reddit accounts who don't have karma yet, I've got a list of over 200 subreddits that need zero karma
I've got a giant list that I sat and made it myself. The list has subreddits for every single niche you want to run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no subreddit list or Reddit content distribution infrastructure (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline is IG/TikTok-centric. The concept of a niche-matched content distribution list is directly analogous to Palm's inspo-source list per creator (§1), but no Reddit equivalent exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Before launching Reddit for any creator, build or acquire a niche-segmented subreddit list and a zero-karma starter list. These can be stored as Airtable records linked to creator niches, enabling VAs to pull the right list at account creation without research overhead. This is a high-leverage, one-time build.

new_findings.title: Create X accounts on mobile data, add Premium immediately to avoid limits

variants.claim: Twitter is pay-to-win in 2026 — every account must have Premium or reach is severely limited.
variants.what_they_do: Accounts are created on mobile with cellular data (never Wi-Fi). Bought aged accounts get Premium added immediately; fresh accounts wait 2–3 days before adding Premium. Every account in the network has Premium regardless of role.

sources.quote: we make them on mobile with data, never Wi-Fi
premium goes on every account. Twitter is paid to win now. If you don't have it, you're just limited

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented X/Twitter traffic strategy (§5 🔲 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). No internal baseline for account creation on X or Premium tier usage.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm pursues X traffic, establish a policy: create accounts on mobile data, apply Premium on day 1 (aged) or day 3 (fresh). Budget Premium cost into traffic CAC math per creator before spinning up X presence.

new_findings.title: Four-bucket fan taxonomy: Lurkers, Average Buyers, Whales, Validation Seekers

variants.claim: Every fan on an OF account falls into one of four identifiable buckets, and the chatter's primary job is to classify each fan quickly and apply the correct strategy per bucket.
variants.what_they_do: Agency trains chatters to identify: (1) Time-wasters/lurkers — flag them in the CRM, mute, deprioritize; (2) Average buyers ($50–$150/month) — build trust and retain; (3) Whales/big spenders — actively develop from average fans via emotional investment, noting that whales can be 50% of total monthly revenue; (4) Validation seekers — sell non-explicit upsells (video calls, letters, VIP packages) framed as emotional rewards. Classification happens at the start of every conversation and is stored in the CRM (Inflow).

sources.quote: one the type of fan that you are going to encounter is time wasters or lurkers
wills and big spenders can contribute 50% of the total monthly revenue

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's whale-hunting module (§9) tracks big spenders and has a Fan Tracker CRM, so fan tiering exists in data. However, Palm has no documented chatter-facing taxonomy or SOP for classifying fans in real-time during DM sessions, and the chatting itself is outsourced (§8). This four-bucket framework would be foundational for Palm's planned in-house chat team and AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, adopt a formal four-bucket fan classification system as the first thing chatters are trained on. Create a CRM field/tag per bucket in Airtable or whatever chat CRM is used. Define escalation triggers: e.g., 'early spend + emotional openness = flag as whale candidate.' This directly feeds Palm's existing whale-hunting infrastructure (§9).

new_findings.topic: Chat funnel architecture — sale-first then nurture
new_findings.title: Flip the funnel: qualify buyers fast, then invest in relationship — not the reverse

variants.claim: The highest-profit chatting funnel prioritizes getting the first sale as quickly as possible after minimal rapport, then builds deep emotional connection only with confirmed buyers — not before.
variants.what_they_do: Their funnel is: awareness → connection/rapport → drop guard → first sale (as fast as possible) → nurture obsession → retain. The key inversion: most agencies build deep connection first and sell later, wasting hours on non-buyers. This agency gets a qualifying sale first, then invests heavily in fans who have proven they will spend. Non-buyers (lurkers) are deprioritized immediately. They explicitly say spending 30–60 minutes building deep connection with a non-buyer is a loss.

sources.quote: we actually flip one part of the funnel to drive even stronger results
your first real goal is to identify who is a buyer and get that initial sale fast

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatting SOP exists at Palm (§8, outsourced). This is a high-confidence build-toward finding for Palm's in-house chat roadmap. The principle also has implications for how Palm briefs its current external chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode this funnel as the core structural SOP for Palm's in-house chat team build-out. Define a 'qualification threshold' (e.g., first PPV unlock or first tip) as the gate before a fan receives deep-investment chatting. Brief current external chat team on this framework as a near-term action to test whether it improves LTV metrics visible in Palm's Fan Tracker (§9).

new_findings.title: Mandatory fan CRM notes with bonus eligibility tied to compliance — 10 misses = no bonus

variants.claim: Detailed per-fan CRM notes (name, location, job, preferences, upsells attempted/sold, relationship context) are enforced through a hard incentive rule: chatters who skip notes 10+ times in a month lose bonus eligibility.
variants.what_they_do: Using Inflow as their chat CRM, chatters log fan info in real-time during conversations: name, location, job, content preferences, past unlocks, emotional context. Notes compound over time and enable any chatter to pick up a conversation seamlessly. The enforcement mechanism is concrete: 10 missed note sessions = no bonus that month. They also say: when auditing underperforming accounts, the first thing they check is whether detailed notes exist on whales — if not, they immediately know money is being left on the table.

sources.quote: if a chatter skips this 10 times in a month they lose the eligibility for bonuses
we immediately check are their detail notes on the whales the big spenders and if they aren't we already know that agency is leaving tons of money on the table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module (§9) that collects spending data, but no documented SOP for real-time conversational note-taking by chatters, and no enforcement mechanism. The chatting is currently outsourced (§8), so Palm cannot currently enforce this — but it is a critical design requirement for the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design the in-house chat team's CRM note schema now (before hiring chatters), covering at minimum: fan name, location, job, content preferences, relationship stage, upsells attempted/sold. Build a bonus structure where note compliance is a gate — not just sales volume. Evaluate whether the current external chat team can be required to log structured notes into Palm's existing Fan Tracker.

new_findings.topic: Chatter hiring criteria, screening process, and compensation structure
new_findings.title: Hire for communication + typing speed + psychology intuition; screen via role-play form; pay 10% pure commission

variants.claim: Any person with strong communication, fast typing, and basic psychological intuition can be trained to elite-level chatting in 1–3 weeks if screened correctly and put on pure commission alignment.
variants.what_they_do: Three hard requirements: (1) strong English/communication; (2) fast typing speed (response target: under 5 minutes, ideally under 2); (3) baseline understanding that emotion drives sales. Intangible green flags: natural rapport-builder, curiosity about psychology, prior sales experience, hunger/drive. Sourcing: Upwork, OnlineJobs.ph, Telegram OFM groups (warn: screenshot fraud common there), personal referrals, own personal brand. Screening: Tally/Google form with basic info + scenario role-plays showing real fan messages — evaluate for naturalness, sales awareness, human-sounding responses. This step eliminates 80% of low-quality applicants. Compensation: agency now runs 100% pure commission at 10% of sales — no base pay. Rationale: protects downside on non-performers, aligns incentives, self-filters lazy chatters. Previous hourly/base structures cost the agency 'tens of thousands' in losses from underperforming chatters.

sources.quote: anyone with right fundamentals can be trained to a top tier chatter in one to two weeks
we personally favor now healthy commissions… 10% of sales is what they get

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatters yet (§8, outsourced; §10, no hiring criteria documented). This is a direct build-toward spec for Palm's ~1-year-out in-house chat team. The 10% pure commission model is a concrete starting point for Palm's compensation design. The role-play screening form is immediately replicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build the chatter hiring funnel now in preparation for the in-house chat team: create a Tally screening form with 3–4 real fan message scenarios, define the three hard requirements as knockout criteria, and set 10% pure commission as the default structure. Start sourcing from Upwork and personal referrals. Do not use hourly-only pay — the math consistently fails. Consider a small performance bonus layer (e.g., upsell bonuses) on top of base commission to incentivize specific behaviors Palm wants to reward.

new_findings.topic: Chat team performance management — tracking, leaderboards, feedback loops
new_findings.title: Three-part chatter performance system: Inflow tracking + weekly scorecards + daily win-sharing Telegram channel

variants.claim: Chatter performance must be tracked with data (response time, PPV unlock ratio, sales per chatter per day), graded weekly on a scorecard beyond just sales, and reinforced daily through a shared Telegram win-sharing channel with cash bonuses.
variants.what_they_do: Tracking via Inflow: message logs per chatter, response time (5-min max flagged, 2-min ideal), PPV unlock-to-message-sent ratio, daily sales per chatter. Weekly: lead chatter or manager reviews stats and gives tailored feedback ('your response time is low, your connection score is weak') — never generic criticism. Scorecard metrics beyond revenue: quality of fan connections built, number and price of upsells sold, retention metrics (whale development), response time. Leaderboards + weekly cash prizes + public shoutouts. Daily: Telegram group where chatters share wins (specific tactic that worked, a turnaround moment, a big upsell) — incentivized with bonuses and shoutouts. Lead chatters shadow junior chatters in first weeks and give tactical notes (missed upsells, flat emotional tone, poor baiting, weak fan loops, premature selling).

sources.quote: we require chatters to respond 5 minutes or less… if it's over 5 minutes, it flags at least a red flag
a telegram group where each member of a team is again incentivized to share their wins whether it's they did a specific tactic that worked

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chat team tracking (§8, outsourced; §9, data collection live but no retention/performance strategy documented). This is a complete operational template for Palm's future in-house chat team. The Telegram win-sharing culture mirrors Palm's existing Telegram coordination infrastructure (§5), making adoption straightforward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design this three-part system as the management layer for the in-house chat team before hiring begins. Immediately: create the scorecard template with both revenue and non-revenue metrics (connection quality, upsell rate, response time, whale development). When the team is live, launch a dedicated Telegram channel for daily win-sharing with a defined bonus for posting wins. Ensure the CRM selected for chatting (whether Inflow or integrated into Airtable) surfaces per-chatter metrics. Also worth implementing a version of this for the current external chat team as a quality-monitoring step.

new_findings.title: Segment creators into 'talented' vs 'untalented' pools and match your system to the tier

variants.claim: Most agencies fail because they apply a talent-dependent marketing system to creators who are only 50% of the quality equation, when a different system is needed for that creator tier.
variants.what_they_do: Jason explicitly splits the creator market into two pools: a small, high-barrier 'talented' pool (highly attractive, great content creator, speaks English, delivers on time) and a large, low-barrier 'untalented' pool. He deliberately targets the untalented pool because it is easier to sign and larger in volume. Before deploying any marketing system, he assesses how many of the four 'model input units' a creator actually brings, then routes her to the matching system (traditional OFM if 4/4, BFAM if 2/4).

sources.quote: four units of model input. We have what's called highly attractive. We have great content creator, speaks English, and delivers content on time.
it's dramatically easier to sign clients from this pool because there's just so many and plus the barrier to entry is very low.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outreach with no documented qualification scorecard or tiering framework (§6). We have no written criteria for which creators get which treatment. Palm implicitly handles a range of creator quality but has no explicit routing logic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 4-point creator qualification scorecard at onboarding (attractiveness tier, content-creation skill, English fluency, content delivery reliability). Score each creator 0–4 and use the score to decide which marketing and content system she enters. Document this in the onboarding wizard and Airtable 'Creators' table.

new_findings.title: BFAM: Build a fully agency-owned traffic and posting system so creator talent is not a dependency

variants.claim: By making the agency responsible for all traffic generation, account management, posting, and distribution — and having creators only upload raw content to a drive — the agency can achieve $10K+ months with average creators.
variants.what_they_do: Jason's BFAM (Brute Force Agency Marketing) system removes creator involvement from everything except raw content upload. The agency runs several accounts, manages all posting and distribution, uses AI to generate marketing content, and controls the full funnel. Creator input is reduced to uploading files to a shared drive a few hours per week. This lets the agency scale horizontally across 10+ creators without being bottlenecked by any individual creator's talent or availability.

sources.quote: we run several accounts and manage all the posting and distribution of the marketing content. It means we manage every inch of the process and creators only upload to the drive removing any friction or input needed from the model side.
we also use AI to generate a lot of the marketing content that we use, which again removes a lot of the friction from the model side.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already operates in this direction — social presence (IG/TikTok), content ideation pipeline (§1), and posting/scheduling (§5) are agency-run. Creators film but the agency decides what they film (inspo pipeline). However, Palm's system is most mature on the content ideation side; the 'run several accounts per creator' and AI-generated marketing content layer is not fully documented. Palm's posting is partially manual via Amin (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize and document Palm's BFAM-equivalent: explicitly state that the agency controls all traffic accounts, posting, and distribution and that creators only upload to the drive. Use this as a selling point in creator acquisition. Also explore AI-generated marketing asset creation (matching Jason's 'AI to generate marketing content') to reduce creator friction further, especially for lower-talent creators.

new_findings.topic: Horizontal vs vertical agency scaling strategy
new_findings.title: Choose horizontal scale (many average creators) over vertical scale (few talented creators) when talent access is limited

variants.claim: Scaling horizontally across 10+ average creators using a brute-force agency system outperforms trying to scale 1–2 talented creators vertically when the agency cannot reliably sign top-tier talent.
variants.what_they_do: Jason consciously chose to scale to 10+ creators horizontally rather than go deep on 1–2 high-talent creators. He frames this as a deliberate strategic decision based on his capabilities and creator access. With BFAM, each creator is less individually dependent on talent, making horizontal addition of new (average) creators a reliable growth lever. He acknowledges this is not universally correct — the right choice depends on the agency's strengths and creator access.

sources.quote: instead of scaling one or two creators vertically, we've decided to scale over a dozen creators.
It all depends on your strengths, your client, and your capabilities.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently works with multiple real creators plus AI personas, which is implicitly horizontal (§0). However, Palm has no documented scaling strategy or written rationale for horizontal vs vertical. The decision framework for how many creators to take on, at what point to add vs. deepen, is absent.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit scaling philosophy: are we horizontal (volume of creators) or vertical (depth per creator)? Given Palm's AI-persona scaling lever (§3) and agency-run systems (§1, §5), horizontal is likely the right model. Write a one-page scaling framework that sets a target creator count, the minimum system infrastructure per creator, and the revenue threshold at which a creator graduates from intensive agency support to steady-state.

new_findings.title: Install a system to capture proof continuously

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

sources.quote: start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but has no documented system for capturing creator-facing wins/testimonials as marketing proof (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a lightweight proof-capture pipeline (Airtable table or Drive) that logs creator wins and testimonials, feeding both the acquisition funnel and a credibility library.

new_findings.title: Personal/agency brand is the real acquisition constraint

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no agency/founder brand or content presence for acquisition (§6 THIN); every Josh DM starts from zero trust.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have Josh (or a Palm-brand channel) publish consistent niche authority content so inbound trust precedes outreach, reducing reliance on cold DMs.

new_findings.title: Volume, clarity, and proof drive content performance

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social engine is data-driven on what to film (§1 MATURE) but cadence/frequency is not codified (§1 GAP) and messaging clarity/proof are not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a high-volume posting cadence in the SMM hub (§11) and add avatar-language and proof guidelines to the content pipeline, complementing Palm's inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Delegate menial work to protect creative output

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

sources.quote: you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already delegates manual posting to a contractor (Amin, §5) and editing to editors (§1), but has no formalized delegation/hiring plan (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a delegation ladder (what the founder keeps vs hands off) and hiring criteria, building on Palm's existing posting/editing outsourcing.

new_findings.title: Track the full outreach funnel and fix one stage at a time

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

sources.quote: after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach funnel metrics (reply/booking/show/close) or follow-up cadence (§6 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Stand up an outbound tracker (reply, qualified, booked, showed, closed) and a multi-touch follow-up sequence, mirroring the data-driven discipline Palm already applies to content.

new_findings.title: Use proven evergreen content verticals, not niches/trends

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

sources.quote: We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm decides what to film from viral inspo data (§1 MATURE) but does not codify reusable evergreen verticals or a per-day posting cadence (§1 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a small set of evergreen, personality-led verticals and a high daily posting cadence in the SMM hub, layered on top of Palm's existing inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Weekly cadence and standards prevent regression to chaos

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

sources.quote: Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm coordinates via Telegram with no documented operating cadence, scorecards, or standards-enforcement rhythm (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Install a lightweight weekly cadence (priorities reset + one root-cause review) and a recognition/consequence standard across Palm's team and contractors.

new_findings.title: Source creators from OF-video comment sections and push to a call

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is outbound-DM + referral but with no documented sourcing method, message template, or call-booking step (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a sourcing playbook (comment-section mining of help-seeking creators), a curiosity-led opener, multi-platform outreach, and call-booking as the CTA.

new_findings.title: Charge no upfront or hidden fees; earn only on a partnership split

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm produces a contract PDF at onboarding but has no documented fee-structure stance (§7 GAP); this is a concrete trust-positioning rule to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 'no upfront, no hidden fees, partner-not-asset' as explicit Palm acquisition trust positioning.

new_findings.title: Build a fan profile through scripted questions before any PPV

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

sources.quote: what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but no chatting scripts or data-capture SOP (§8 OUTSOURCED).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pair Palm's fan CRM with a scripted data-collection intake and a build-up PPV ladder for the in-house/AI chat build.

new_findings.topic: systemize then scale one creator at a time
new_findings.title: Systemize day-one onboarding and perfect one creator before scaling

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is multi-creator past this stage, but the 'systemize before you scale' discipline maps onto Palm's THIN/GAP areas (acquisition §6, pricing §7, retention §9) that aren't yet documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply 'document and systemize before delegating/scaling' to close Palm's THIN areas before further growth.

new_findings.title: Let AI sort and convert new subs; route spenders to human chatters

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

palm_comparison.vs_us: Defines the target architecture for Palm's AI-chatting project and future in-house team (§8 build-toward): AI for volume/sorting, humans for whale retention.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's chat roadmap as a hybrid: AI for new-sub volume and spender-sorting, human chatters owning small VIP client lists for retention, with unlock-rate as a tracked KPI.

new_findings.title: Use an Intrigue-Curiosity-CTA opener and book the call fast

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented DM script, send-rate discipline, or follow-up cadence for acquisition (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ICC opener template, a manual-typed/personalized/spaced sending SOP to avoid bans, and a 2-week daily follow-up rule; track per-step conversion.

new_findings.title: Qualify creators against a checklist and disqualify on red flags

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

sources.quote: Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding captures admin fields but has no documented fit scorecard or red-flag screen (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an ideal-client checklist plus a red-flag screen to the onboarding/intake step so Palm signs for fit and retention, not just availability.

new_findings.title: Anchor on a 50% full-service split, don't undersell at 20-30%

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale/tiers (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's commission rationale with a 50% default anchor and explicit guidance on when to deviate, so splits aren't negotiated ad hoc.

new_findings.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV script ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

new_findings.title: Build content around the creator's niche and her own strengths

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is data-driven and mature for ideation (§1), but niche/positioning and creator-strength fit are not explicit fields in the flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a niche + creator-strengths step feeding the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas are filtered for fit, not just virality.

new_findings.title: Spin up backup/secondary accounts and post across many platforms

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution strategy (Reddit, multi-account, S4S) is an explicit GAP (§5); cadence is partially uncodified (§1). Note: this overlaps Palm's AI warmup multi-account capability (§3/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-account/multi-platform traffic strategy with tracking links for attribution and a week-ahead scheduling cadence; reuse AI-warmup account infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Onboard with a documented same-day process and a welcome video

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding is a form wizard → Creators table (§6); strong on contract automation but no documented expectation-setting welcome video or same-day activation SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a standardized welcome video + same-day activation checklist to onboarding to set expectations and cut early churn.

new_findings.title: Run three departments (acquisition, marketing, chatting) each under a manager

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm org is THIN (§10): no management layer between founders and ICs, no org chart.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a department-and-manager org (acquisition / marketing / chatting) with the founder managing through managers as Palm scales past the current flat structure.

new_findings.title: Use the fan's name, kill mass-messages

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but personalization data and tone live with outsourced chatters (§8). Build-toward plus owning the data.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Ensure Palm's own per-fan CRM captures the name and interests, and bar generic mass-message bundle blasts, so personalization survives any chatting-vendor change and feeds future AI chat.

new_findings.topic: Revenue vs profit distinction at scale
new_findings.title: High revenue means nothing without margin — $100k revenue can yield $3k profit

variants.claim: Agencies can hit $100,000/month in revenue while netting as little as $3,000 in profit, so raw revenue targets are misleading without tracking margin.
variants.what_they_do: TDM explicitly warns against 'brute forcing' to $50–70k/month and treating it as success; they frame margin as the true health metric, arguing that low-margin agencies cannot reinvest in better team, systems, or experimentation, which caps growth.

sources.quote: some agencies will do $100,000 in revenue and $3,000 in profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented internal baseline for margin targets or profit-vs-revenue tracking (§7 GAP). Weekly invoices are auto-computed but there is no documented P&L framework or margin floor that triggers a scaling decision.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a minimum margin % threshold (e.g., 30–40% net) as an internal KPI alongside gross revenue. Build a simple P&L view into the admin portal or Airtable so Josh and Evan can see whether revenue growth is actually profitable before committing to new hires or infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Take a week off at $100k/month to expose founder dependency before it becomes a ceiling

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is early-stage with Josh leading acquisition and strategy hands-on (§6). No documented test for founder dependency exists. Given the current scale, this is a forward-looking tool, but the diagnostic framework is immediately useful to plan against.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Schedule a planned 5-day founder-absence drill (even a partial one — Josh steps back from one department at a time) to surface which processes break. Use the results to prioritize which workflows need SOPs or a designated owner first. Document the gaps in Airtable.

new_findings.title: Convert founder gut-reactions into SOPs so the team executes your standards without you

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

sources.quote: if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's systems are strong on the content pipeline (§1) but thin on creator-facing ops, chatting protocols, and acquisition handling (§6, §8). The SOP layer for creator management and issue resolution is not documented internally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Run a structured 'intuition capture' session: Josh walks through the 10 most common creator/fan/operational situations he handles and dictates how he'd resolve each. Evan documents these as v1 SOPs in Airtable or Notion. Prioritize creator onboarding issues, escalation handling, and any workflow currently bottlenecked on Josh.

new_findings.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10): Amin is a contractor, editors exist, and an external chat team is outsourced. No documented incentive structure or performance-linked comp bands exist. This is a GAP as Palm begins building in-house teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When hiring the first in-house chatters (§8 roadmap) and any additional editors or marketing staff, design role-specific KPIs with a variable comp component tied to those KPIs (e.g., chatters earn a bonus % of PPV revenue above a baseline; editors earn a bonus per post that hits a reach threshold). Document comp bands in the org structure build-out.

new_findings.title: Use 'what breaks if revenue doubles tomorrow' as the primary systems-gap diagnostic

variants.claim: The most practical way to identify scaling bottlenecks is to ask 'what would break if our revenue doubled overnight' rather than asking 'how do we grow.'
variants.what_they_do: TDM frames the scaling question as a stress-test thought experiment: list every department and workflow, then identify which ones would snap under 2x load. The output is a prioritized list of systems, hires, and automations to build before growth demands them, not after.

sources.quote: what would happen and what would break if revenue doubled tomorrow

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is in an active SMM hub redesign (§11) and has several in-flight initiatives. There is no documented stress-test exercise or pre-mortem for scaling. Given that Palm is building infrastructure now, this lens is immediately applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Conduct a 'double revenue' war-game session: map every current operational workflow and ask what would break at 2x creator count or 2x content volume. Use the output to sequence the remaining build plan — prioritize fixes to workflows that would break first (likely: content pipeline throughput, Amin/posting bottleneck, creator onboarding, and the chat team handoff).

new_findings.topic: Incremental hiring in step with revenue milestones
new_findings.title: Add team members and build systems in proportion to revenue growth — not all at once

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

sources.quote: 10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring and org structure are thin and undocumented (§10 GAP). There is no scaling roadmap that ties headcount or system builds to specific revenue milestones. Palm risks the 'brute force' trap the video warns against if growth outpaces internal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a simple revenue-milestone roadmap in Airtable or Notion: at each $X increment in monthly revenue, define the next hire, SOP, or system to be built. Even a rough version (e.g., at $X add a second editor; at $Y formalize creator KPI reporting; at $Z begin in-house chat hiring) provides the incremental discipline TDM describes.

new_findings.topic: Multi-account content rotation — avoiding identical Reels grids
new_findings.title: Rotate which repurposed video goes to which account — never mirror the same grid

variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

sources.quote: if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current scheduling system (§5) routes posts via Publer or Telegram/Amin but is designed for one primary account per creator. There is no documented logic for distributing content across a cluster of repurposing accounts for a single creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a distribution-rotation rule in the scheduling layer: for any creator running N repurposing accounts, assign each daily video to a unique subset of those accounts via a round-robin or modular assignment in Airtable/Publer. This prevents grid mirroring and reduces ban risk.

new_findings.topic: Organic vs. repurposed account architecture — strict separation
new_findings.title: Never mix organic and repurposed content on the same IG account

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

sources.quote: you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) is entirely organic/creator-filmed for the primary account. Palm does not currently run a repurposing-account layer. This finding clarifies the correct architecture IF Palm adds one: keep the existing organic pipeline untouched and stand up a separate repurposing operation alongside it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm scales to multi-account reposting, codify the two-tier rule in the SMM hub redesign (§11): tag accounts as 'organic-primary' or 'repurpose-only' in Airtable, and enforce via posting-workflow routing so editors never schedule repurposed content to an organic-primary account.

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: Never mention OnlyFans by name on social — use clean indirect CTAs

variants.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
