variants.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.

sources.quote: do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces social posts but has no documented compliance rules around OF mention avoidance or shadowban risk on IG/TikTok. This is a platform-risk gap (§ platform-risk) that could silently harm creator accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a compliance rule to Palm's content/posting SOP: no direct OF platform name mentions in captions, audio, or on-screen text on IG or TikTok. Standardize CTA language (e.g., 'link in bio,' 'exclusive content,' 'join my page') across all creator accounts. Include this as a checklist item in the editor approval workflow.

new_findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

new_findings.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

sources.quote: set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or QBR rhythm (§9 flags this as a gap). Communication is through Telegram per §5. There is no standing weekly call structure with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a standing weekly 20-min creator check-in call. Build a lightweight call template in Notion: (1) last-week metrics summary pulled from OF dashboard, (2) content requests for next week, (3) any platform/account issues, (4) open Q&A. Log call notes back to the Creators Airtable record. This directly fills the §9 retention/churn gap.

new_findings.title: Use the onboarding process itself as a passive screening tool for creator quality and reliability

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) — it captures data but generates no behavioral signal about the creator. There is no documented creator-quality scoring or early-stage filtering criteria.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 30-day post-onboarding creator quality review field to the Creators Airtable table. Score creators on: (1) content submission timeliness, (2) responsiveness on Telegram, (3) implementation of feedback from first weekly call. Use scores to prioritize agency resource allocation and, as the roster scales, to inform future signing decisions.

new_findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

new_findings.title: Always wrap up a sexting session before logging off to prevent churn

variants.claim: Abruptly ending a chat session after a subscriber has spent money makes them feel used and dramatically reduces the chance they return or spend again.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly instructed to close every sexting/monetization session with a proper conversational wrap-up — warm sign-off, leaving the subscriber feeling positive and wanting to return. Logging off mid-session is a fireable-level error in the VA training.

sources.quote: always wrap up the conversation sexting session before logging off this is to leave the subscriber feeling good and wanting to come back for more next time otherwise if you abruptly stop replying the subscriber has spent hundreds of dollars on content they're going to feel used

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP. This 'session closure' practice is a key retention mechanic that should be codified in Palm's future in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt instructions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'session closure protocol' to the chatter SOP: before ending any active conversation, send a warm closing message (e.g. affirmation, teaser for next time). Make this a scored QA checkpoint when reviewing chatter performance.

new_findings.title: Send a single sad-face emoji to re-engage non-responding subscribers

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

sources.quote: if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented re-engagement tactic (§8 outsourced, §9 partial fan retention). This is a high-signal, zero-cost tactic for the in-house chat roadmap and is also relevant to AI chatting logic (trigger sad emoji after N hours of non-response).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this into the chatter re-engagement SOP: if subscriber has not replied after 24–48 hours, send a single sad-face emoji (no text). Log the response rate in Fan Tracker CRM to validate. For AI chatting, add as an automated trigger: no-reply after configurable timeout → send sad emoji → await response before any further action.

new_findings.title: Never send multiple follow-up messages to a non-responding subscriber

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat SOP (§8 outsourced). If the external team lacks this discipline it may be causing churn Palm can't see. This rule should be a core constraint in both the in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt guardrails.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a hard rule to the chatter SOP and AI chatting system prompt: after one unanswered message, no further text messages until the subscriber replies (only the sad-face emoji exception is permitted). Make this a QA audit criterion when reviewing chatter logs.

new_findings.title: Length-based PPV pricing ladder: $5–$8 short, $8–$20 long, nothing under 1 min

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 — #1 gap flagged). No internal baseline exists for price ladders, anchoring, or length-based rules. This is pure learn-from-others territory.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default PPV pricing framework for all Palm creators: <1 min = free/engagement only; 1–3 min = $5–$8; 3–8 min = $8–$15; 8+ min = $15–$25. Document as a pricing SOP and share with the external chat team so they apply consistent pricing in DMs. Review per-creator after 30 days and adjust based on conversion data already in the system.

new_findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

new_findings.title: Golden Ratio: 60 DMs/day for 30 days guarantees first client

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

new_findings.title: Cross-platform username matching: find OF creators on X, then locate on IG

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

sources.quote: you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented prospect-sourcing method beyond Josh's personal DMs and referrals (§6). No cross-platform lookup or IG graph-traversal tactic is codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a prospect-sourcing SOP using both methods: (1) X keyword search → IG username lookup, and (2) IG related-accounts traversal from any known OF creator. This is low-cost and immediately actionable for whoever runs outreach at Palm.

new_findings.title: Escalating PPV price ladder: free photo → $15 → $50 → $100 → $150 → $200

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

sources.quote: the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP, scripts, or pricing ladder documented. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team roadmap and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this escalating PPV ladder as the foundational script template for Palm's future in-house chat team. Specifically: define free-teaser → low-anchor PPV → mid-tier PPV → high-tier PPV price points; have creators pre-shoot content sequences in order before chatters launch; use South Africa as primary chatter sourcing market for English-speaking creator accounts. Brief the external chat team on whether they already use this structure.

new_findings.title: Do chatting yourself for 2 weeks before hiring, then hire South African chatters for cultural fluency

variants.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
variants.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

sources.quote: step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting entirely today (§8); this tactic is build-toward for the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap). No internal SOP exists for chatter hiring geography or shift structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, use this framework: (1) have the team lead personally chat for 2 weeks per creator to develop training materials, (2) prioritize South African candidates for English-language creator accounts, (3) structure 3-chatter rotating 8-hour shifts per active creator account. Document this in the chat-team hiring SOP before recruitment begins.

new_findings.topic: AI chatbot displacement of human chatters
new_findings.title: AI chatbots will soon displace low-cost human chatters — build AI chat now

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

sources.quote: AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's roadmap already includes an AI chatting project (§8), which directly anticipates this shift. This finding is high-confidence validation that Palm's AI chat roadmap is correctly timed and should be accelerated rather than deprioritized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this as justification to prioritize the AI chatting roadmap project. When evaluating build vs. buy, note that the window to build a proprietary advantage is narrowing as third-party AI chat vendors improve. Capture competitor AI chat implementations (scripts, fan-tier logic, PPV sequencing) as build-toward intelligence per §8.

new_findings.title: Multi-stage hiring funnel with hard filters before any offer

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

sources.quote: set up a direct hiring funnel that may even take like a week to get all the applicants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring is not formally documented (§10 — THIN). There is no written hiring funnel, scorecard, or onboarding SOP. Josh currently drives acquisition and ops largely himself, which matches the 'doing it yourself while you find the right person' fallback, but the structured multi-stage filter does not exist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a formal 2–3 stage hiring funnel for each role Palm needs next (e.g., editor, chatter, VA): job post → async task/test → live screen → offer. Define a written 'must-have' scorecard per role before opening any search. Document the cost-of-bad-hire math internally so the team doesn't rush to fill seats.

new_findings.title: Daily feedback cadence to accelerate employee ramp 7x vs weekly

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

sources.quote: give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented feedback cadence for editors, Amin, or any contractor (§10 — THIN). There are no performance review cycles or KPI dashboards per employee in the codebase. The closest analogue is the editor task queue in the portal, which surfaces output but not a feedback loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a lightweight daily async check-in for each active role (editor, posting contractor, eventually chatters): a shared Telegram thread or Airtable comment thread where the reviewer flags one specific 'fix this' and one 'good job' per day per person. Formalise this before hiring the in-house chat team (~1 yr roadmap item, §8).

new_findings.title: Run 3-variant script tests with unlock-rate tracking before standardising any chatting SOP

variants.claim: Running three concurrent script variants with measured unlock rates — rather than just deploying a new script agency-wide — reveals the specific element driving performance and prevents agencies from unknowingly using inferior scripts.
variants.what_they_do: When a new chatting script is introduced, the agency assigns variant A, B, and C to different chatters or conversation batches, tracks PPV unlock rate per variant, diagnoses why the winning variant beat the others (e.g., opening line framing), and only then canonises the best approach across all chatters.

sources.quote: test every single one of them. Okay, this script got a 50% unlock rate. The other variation is 30%, this is 55. Okay, why?

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is currently outsourced at Palm (§8 — OUTSOURCED); no internal script management or test infrastructure exists today. This is a build-toward finding for the planned in-house and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing Palm's in-house chat SOP (§8 roadmap), build variant testing into the process architecture from day one: store script variants in Airtable with a linked 'test result' table capturing unlock rate and revenue per test run. Do not import a competitor's script wholesale — always test 3 variants first.

new_findings.title: Structured 5-step onboarding checklist for new creator sign-ons

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form-wizard onboarding that captures name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo (§6), but no documented post-signing operational checklist covering OF account audit, vault organization, social setup, or expectations management. The onboarding stops at the contract layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard with a post-contract ops checklist: (1) OF login + 2FA coordination, (2) subscriber spend-sort, (3) vault audit/organization, (4) social platform gap-fill, (5) content schedule delivery, (6) expectations call with month-1/2/3 benchmarks. Build this as a checklist state-machine in the Creators table or a dedicated onboarding UI.

new_findings.title: Match viral inspo accounts to creator's look-type before briefing

variants.claim: Sending creators viral content from models who look like them — same hair color, body type, vibe — dramatically increases the likelihood the creator will replicate it well and that the content will resonate with the right audience.
variants.what_they_do: Agency manually finds a model whose look closely matches the new creator (e.g., 'blonde cheerleader type'), identifies that model's currently-viral videos, and sends them as direct filming references. Creator replicates the format/dance/hook on IG and TikTok.

sources.quote: try and find her ideas for Content that are similar to her looking so if she's a blond kind of cheerleader type model then fantastic find another model who looks like her and is currently going viral

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) scrapes inspo from a defined list of accounts and uses AI vision to score virality, but the step of matching inspo sources to a specific creator's look-type is not documented. Inspo may be pooled generically across creators rather than look-matched. This is a refinement opportunity inside the existing pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'look profile' field to the Creators table (hair color, body type, vibe tags). When a new creator onboards, seed their Inspo Sources list with accounts that match their look profile rather than only genre/category. The existing scrape + AI-score pipeline then handles the rest.

new_findings.title: Use US SIM / US-based poster for non-English-speaking creators to avoid low-spend audiences

variants.claim: If a creator posts TikTok from a country with weak purchasing power (e.g., Colombia), viral reach fills her funnel with followers who cannot afford OF subscriptions — a US SIM card or US proxy poster fixes the audience geography.
variants.what_they_do: For creators based in Latin America or other non-Tier-1 countries, agency either ships/provides a US SIM card so the creator's device registers as US-based, or recruits a US-based person to post on her behalf, ensuring TikTok's algorithm surfaces content to US audiences.

sources.quote: you would not really my advice would not be for you to get her to post it herself on Tik Tok because you'll if she goes viral she'll get a bunch of Colombian followers and the currency exchange doesn't really make sense

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a hardware-isolated posting system for AI accounts (§3/§4) with dedicated SIMs per persona, but no documented geo-routing strategy for real creators posting from non-Tier-1 countries. This gap could be costing subscriber conversion rates for any internationally-based Palm creators. (§5 gap)
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit Palm's current real-creator roster for location. For any creator outside Tier-1 English-speaking markets, evaluate whether their TikTok posts are geo-targeting US/UK audiences. If not, explore US SIM provisioning using the existing SIM Inventory infrastructure (§3) or route their posts through Amin with a US-based device.

new_findings.topic: Pre-loaded chat scripts with embedded media
new_findings.title: Equip chatters with pre-written scripts that have photos/videos pre-attached for real-time sends

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: 80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP or script library. This is directly relevant build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and AI chatting project. The key structural insight is media-to-script pre-linking, not just having scripts alone.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat system, design the script library with media assets pre-linked at the node level (not stored separately). Each chat script node should reference a specific Asset ID from the content vault. This prevents chatters from breaking conversation flow to search the vault — a documented friction point in competitor agencies.

new_findings.title: Use carousels as growth drivers, text posts as volume filler on Threads

variants.claim: Carousel posts move the needle most on Threads, but text/comment posts are needed as cheap filler to hit daily volume targets.
variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. Of the 5–8 daily posts, at least a couple are carousels; the rest are short text posts. They prioritise content that is shareable and/or controversial as the single biggest lever.

sources.quote: comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is built around IG Reels and carousels but is not mapped to Threads formats. The inspo-driven pipeline could surface shareable content concepts that apply cross-platform, but there is no Threads-specific content classification.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the content pipeline's format taxonomy to include 'Threads carousel' and 'Threads text post' types. When the AI vision analysis (Step 3) rates a reel high on shareability/controversy, flag it as a Threads carousel candidate as well.

new_findings.title: Scale to 15–20 Threads accounts per creator; bottleneck is manpower and hardware

variants.claim: A single creator can be scaled across up to 15–20 Threads accounts with near-linear compounding returns if content and operational capacity allow.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs multiple Threads accounts per creator (up to 15–20), treating content volume and staff/hardware as the only real constraints. They use a phone farm for IG as the mechanism for managing Threads accounts and explicitly avoid automation tools (schedulers/outlers) due to Meta's sensitivity, preferring manual or native posting.

sources.quote: if you've got enough content and the content performs and you see return on investment, you could run up to 15 20 accounts per creator. The bottleneck here is often times the manpower to actually run that and the hardware

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already operates a hardware-isolated phone farm for AI personas (§3/§4) with per-device SIM and account separation. This infrastructure could extend to Threads multi-account scaling. However, Palm's real-creator social setup (§4 GAP) is not formalised, and multi-account Threads for real creators is not documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Assess whether Palm's existing Pixel/SIM infrastructure (§3) can support Threads multi-account scaling for real creators as well as AI personas. Define a Threads account-per-creator target (start at 3–5, test toward 10+). Add Threads account slots to the `AI Account Profile` or a parallel real-creator table in Airtable.

new_findings.title: Salary creator model: pay creator fixed rate, agency keeps ~90% of revenue

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. Creator has no performance incentive; agency owns all marketing decisions. This is contrasted with rev-share models where creator keeps 70%+ and agency takes ~30%.

sources.quote: I could theoretically be paying this creator $2500 every single month, depending on where she lives, upwards to $10,000 a month. I keep 90% of the profit.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses a commission/revenue-split model set per creator at onboarding (§7) — no documented salary-creator option. Palm's split rationale is not written down (§7 GAP). The salary model is a materially different deal structure Palm has not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'salary creator' tier to Palm's deal structure documentation (§7). Define eligibility criteria (e.g. creator has no existing following; Palm does all marketing) and salary bands by geography. This model makes most sense for Palm's AI personas or for raw-talent real creators with no existing audience, where Palm controls 100% of the funnel.

new_findings.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
new_findings.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

new_findings.title: Segment OF creators into 4 revenue tiers to target the right clients

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented targeting criteria for which creator tier to pursue (§6 GAP). Acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no written segmentation model. This competitor has a concrete framework that tells a new agency owner exactly which creators to approach and why.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a one-page creator segmentation model for Palm: define the four revenue tiers, articulate the ROI argument for each, and codify which tier(s) Palm currently targets and why. Use this as a qualifier in outbound DM scripts so Josh — and eventually any acquisition hire — is fishing in the right pond from day one.

new_findings.title: Proactively explain 'why' behind every agency decision to prevent creator churn from miscommunication

variants.claim: Most creator-agency relationship problems stem from unclear communication about what the agency does and why, not from the tactics themselves.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's top lesson is that agencies fail creators by not explaining the reasoning behind decisions. When a creator complains or pushes back, the wrong response is 'we just do this because it works.' The right response is to genuinely understand what's bothering them — is it the content type? The social media time demand? The lack of transparency? He builds in active listening and clear explanation as a core operational habit, not an ad-hoc fix.

sources.quote: a lot of issues that are going to arise are going to be from you not communicating clearly with your client on like what you do and just setting expectations framing stuff the correct way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator onboarding captures basics (form wizard, contract, voice memo — §6) but there is no documented ongoing communication cadence or SOP for handling creator objections/concerns. As Palm scales, this gap will compound — creators managed at arm's length without regular 'why' explanations will churn faster.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add two communication elements to Palm's creator management SOP: (1) An onboarding 'strategy brief' call where Palm explains the content pipeline, posting cadence, and chatting approach — and why each element works — before anything goes live. (2) A recurring creator check-in (bi-weekly or monthly) with a structured agenda: results review, upcoming content plan, anything the creator is uncomfortable with. Template these in Airtable against the `Creators` table.

new_findings.title: Cross-train all chatters on a single universal persona tone, especially for whale handoffs

variants.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
variants.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.

sources.quote: they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM system (§9) but no internal chatter voice/persona standard (§8, outsourced). For the AI-persona line (§3), Palm already thinks in terms of a consistent synthetic persona — that same discipline needs to carry into the chat layer when brought in-house.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a persona voice guide for each creator before the in-house chat team goes live. Define: vocabulary/slang the creator uses, emotional register, how she responds to high spenders vs. new subs, hard boundaries on what she'd 'say.' Make this guide mandatory reading at chatter onboarding. For AI personas (§3), this guide already partially exists — extract and formalize it.

new_findings.title: Never pause acquisition — run it as a 24/7 autopilot system

variants.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.

sources.quote: the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and effectively pauses when attention shifts to delivery (§6). There is no documented funnel, VSL, or call-setting layer, and acquisition is not yet automated or delegated.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's current outreach method into an SOP, then hire/delegate outreach execution so acquisition runs independently of delivery. Add a funnel layer (lead-magnet video → VSL → booked call) to qualify higher-tier creators before the call. Treat acquisition as an always-on background process, not a sequential phase.

new_findings.title: Stop signing low-tier creators — quality floor determines revenue ceiling

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

sources.quote: if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is outbound-DM-led with no documented quality-scoring rubric for creator tiers (§6). There is no lead-magnet content funnel that pre-qualifies creator quality before outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a minimum creator quality rubric (work ethic signals, existing audience, content output history) and apply it as a gate before onboarding. Produce story-driven case-study content (process videos, not just stat screenshots) to attract self-selecting high-tier creators inbound.

new_findings.title: Assign each creator 2–3 content verticals matched to her strengths, then iterate on viral data

variants.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.

sources.quote: once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline scrapes viral inspo and promotes reels to a creator's board (§1), which is data-driven but inspo-led rather than creator-vertical-led. There is no documented step where a creator is formally assigned 2–3 fixed verticals as a strategic frame before ideation begins.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'vertical assignment' step at creator onboarding: define 2–3 content verticals per creator based on her strengths/interests, then filter inspo scraping (§1 pipeline) to those verticals first. After 6 weeks of posting, run a formal data review to codify which verticals are outperforming, and adjust the inspo source list accordingly.

new_findings.title: Write the SOP before hiring, not after — every role must have a documented replacement path

variants.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.

sources.quote: if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team/org structure is THIN (§10) with no documented org chart, hiring scorecards, or comp bands. Josh currently handles acquisition personally (§6), which Ellis explicitly identifies as a founder bottleneck to eliminate first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Prioritise SOP creation for the two highest-founder-time functions (acquisition outreach and creator onboarding). Write the SOP while doing the job, then hire against it. Model Ellis's reinvestment discipline: define a minimum reinvestment rate of revenue back into team/systems before founder draw, especially during the $30K–$100K scaling phase.

new_findings.title: Train chatters to exceed founder-level performance rather than chatting yourself

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

sources.quote: if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting to an external team today (§8) — this is already structurally aligned with Ellis's recommendation. The build-toward for Palm is in-house chat team training and AI chatting (§8 roadmap). Ellis's training-focus framing is directly applicable to designing the in-house chatter onboarding and quality-ramp process.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds toward an in-house chat team (§8 roadmap), design the programme around a 'train chatters to beat founder level' benchmark rather than a floor-level compliance model. Document what a 10/10 chat interaction looks like, build that into chatter SOPs and QA scoring, and assign a chat manager whose sole job is raising chatter skill — not just monitoring volume.

new_findings.title: Use passive teaser content (towel/shower clips) to make the fan initiate sexual escalation, not the chatter

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

sources.quote: the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced). This is a high-value build-toward mechanic — it maps directly to the psychological structure Palm will need when scripting AI chatting (active roadmap project, §8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: In the in-house/AI chat SOP, codify 'fan-initiated escalation' as a required step between rapport and first PPV. Build a library of passive teaser content categories (shower, waving, casual home setting) and matching neutral captions. For AI chatting specifically, this mechanic is low-risk and high-leverage — the AI never has to make a sexual first move.

new_findings.title: Send blurred/cropped preview before full PPV to spark curiosity and drive unlock

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

sources.quote: use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV sequencing SOP (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is a concrete mechanical step missing from any documented Palm chatting or PPV workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the blur/crop preview as a standard step in the PPV send sequence for both the outsourced chat team (share as guidance) and future in-house SOP. Editors/content team should produce a 'teaser variant' of each PPV asset at upload time to make this operationally easy.

new_findings.title: Triage multi-account chat queues by subscriber spend history, not recency

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but no documented protocol for chat prioritization within the outsourced team. This is a logical extension of the whale-hunting data Palm already collects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Export whale/high-spend fan flags from Palm's Fan Tracker into whatever interface the chat team uses, and formalize a 'respond to these first' rule. This bridges the existing whale-hunting infrastructure (§9) with chat execution — a quick operational win.

new_findings.title: Chatters maintain per-fan notes (age, relationship status, interests) to personalize every session

variants.claim: Chatters who record personal details shared by fans during conversations can use those details in future sessions to create a sense of genuine ongoing relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to jot down fan-disclosed details — name, age, relationship status, hobbies, personal anecdotes — and reference them in later messages. The explicit goal is to make the fan feel 'seen, heard, and valued' rather than like one of many subscribers.

sources.quote: jot down details about them you want to know more than just their name keep track of things like their age relationship status and any personal tibbits they share

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but it is oriented toward spend/whale detection rather than personal-detail logging for chat personalization. The outsourced team's note-taking practices are unknown.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Fan Tracker with a free-text 'fan notes' field (personal details, interests, shared anecdotes) that chatters can read before and write to after each session. This aligns with Palm's existing CRM infrastructure and directly improves re-engagement quality.

new_findings.title: Shift subscriber value prop from visuals to synthetic parasocial attachment

variants.claim: Top agencies do not sell explicit content — they sell synthetic attachment and parasocial engineering, and subscriber drop-off is a retention loop problem, not a traffic problem.
variants.what_they_do: Elite agencies reframe the product as emotional/parasocial dependency rather than explicit content. They diagnose churn as a failure of the retention loop, not insufficient traffic, and build systems around keeping subscribers emotionally tethered for 6+ months.

sources.quote: The top agencies do not sell explicit content. They sell synthetic attachment and parasocial engineering.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's retention work (§9) is partially built — whale-hunting, Fan Tracker CRM, going-cold detection — but there is no documented retention *strategy* or scripted emotional loop. Palm identifies whales but has no written framework for *why* fans stay or what attachment mechanic is being engineered. This is a direct gap (§9 🔲).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a retention loop framework for each Palm creator: define the emotional archetype being sold (not just the content type), and build a chatter SOP (build-toward §8) around deepening parasocial attachment rather than pure PPV pushing. This reframe should inform both the in-house chat roadmap and AI persona persona design (§3).

new_findings.title: Use 'false subordination' opener scripts to activate fan white-knight complex

variants.claim: Opening DM conversations with vulnerability-signaling scripts (e.g. the creator 'waited' for the fan, expresses loneliness) gives the fan an illusion of power and activates a protective/white-knight instinct that increases willingness to spend.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters open with scripted 'false subordination hooks' — the model signals fragility and dependence (oversized sweater, lowered voice, 'waited for you') while the fan believes he is the rescuer. The power dynamic is inverted: the fan feels in control while being monetized per minute.

sources.quote: Do not start conversations with generic greetings. Use false subordination hooks.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8); no internal scripts or openers exist. This is directly relevant as build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and AI chatting project. The false subordination mechanic is a concrete, nameable script pattern to evaluate and potentially include in Palm's future chatter training SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Log this as a named script archetype ('false subordination hook') in Palm's build-toward chatting playbook. When building the in-house chat SOP, evaluate this opener pattern against other approaches. Also assess fit for AI persona DM scripts — the mechanic may be more controllable and consistent at scale with AI chatting.

new_findings.topic: Reddit account creation — mobile data only
new_findings.title: Create Reddit accounts on mobile data, never Wi-Fi

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts on a mobile data plan (never Wi-Fi) is critical to avoiding immediate flagging and bans.
variants.what_they_do: Every account is created fresh on mobile with a cellular data plan. No purchased accounts, no Wi-Fi. Franko tried buying accounts in 2024 and says it did not work. All accounts are self-made from scratch on mobile data.

sources.quote: we make all our accounts on mobile with a data plan and we never touch Wi-Fi

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit account creation process (§5 GAP on traffic/distribution; §platform-risk baseline absent for Reddit). We do have hardware-isolation discipline for AI persona accounts (GrapheneOS, 1 SIM/persona — §3/§4), which shows we understand device/network hygiene, but that discipline has not been applied to Reddit ops.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm runs Reddit traffic for any creator, establish a Reddit account creation SOP mirroring the device/network hygiene already used for AI personas: cellular data only, no shared Wi-Fi, ideally one SIM per account cluster. Do not buy aged accounts.

new_findings.topic: Reddit profile setup — bio, links, pinned posts
new_findings.title: Structured Reddit profile: niche-specific one-line bio, OF link in profile section only, two curiosity-driven pinned posts

variants.claim: A carefully structured Reddit profile — OF link only in the link field (not posts), a niche-specific one-line bio with a downward arrow, and two pinned model-photo posts with curiosity-driving captions — maximizes profile-to-subscription conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Profile banner: tasteful model preview. Bio: one line, niche-matched, arrow pointing down to links. OF link lives in the Reddit profile link section only — never in the post body. Two pinned posts: model photos with captions crafted to create curiosity, not spammy copy. Link naming convention is optimized for click-through.

sources.quote: the actual only fence link is in the link section, not the posts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit profile setup standard documented (§5 GAP). Palm does have strong IG profile/bio discipline as part of the social pipeline (§1), so the concept of conversion-optimized profile copy is familiar, but has not been extended to Reddit.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a Reddit profile setup template for each creator niche: bio formula, link naming convention, and two pinned-post caption templates. Store these as Airtable records under a new 'Reddit Profile' table or as a section within the existing creator onboarding documentation (§6).

new_findings.title: Live chatter training session as onboarding format

variants.claim: Running a live training session is an effective onboarding format for new chatters joining an OFM agency.
variants.what_they_do: The agency hosts a live group session with slides to train chatters, suggesting a structured, synchronous onboarding approach rather than purely async SOPs or recorded content.

sources.quote: I want to take this chance to officially welcome you to our super live session

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team with no documented internal chatter onboarding (§8). This signals competitors are building formal training infrastructure for chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), develop a structured live onboarding format with slide decks as a core training artifact; this video may contain more tactical content if a fuller transcript is available.

new_findings.topic: Paid promo via meme pages on X
new_findings.title: Buy sponsored posts on X meme pages to funnel niche audiences to creator profile

variants.claim: Paid placements on X meme pages are profitable traffic sources when the post style and commentary are matched to the audience.
variants.what_they_do: Identify meme pages on X with relevant niche audiences, pay for a sponsored post, and use specific post styles and commentary framing (not disclosed) to maximize click-through to the creator profile. Used selectively — not the primary method — but described as reliably profitable when executed correctly.

sources.quote: paid promos. We don't do that much, but when we do, they profit. You just find meme pages, pay for a post, funnel people over

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented paid traffic strategy for X or any platform (§5 🔲 GAP). This is a lighter, lower-infrastructure alternative to mother-slave that could be tested quickly without bot infrastructure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Test X meme-page paid promo as a quick, low-infra traffic experiment for one real creator. Budget $100–200 per test, track OF link clicks and sub conversions. If profitable, build a vetted meme-page directory in Airtable by niche. This de-risks X traffic learnings before committing to mother-slave infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Benchmark acquisition funnel: 12 applicants/day → 1 signed/week → 1 keeper/month

variants.claim: Running Facebook and Instagram outreach yields roughly 12 applicants per day, one signing per week, and one high-quality keeper per month — expect high drop-off at every stage.
variants.what_they_do: They run inbound acquisition via Facebook and Instagram ads/outreach generating 12 model applications daily. Of those, ~1/week actually signs a contract, and of ~4/month signed, only 1 performs well enough to retain. They use a legal contract template adapted to both the agency's and model's country of residence.

sources.quote: we get about 12 models a day applying and of those 12 models we get about one per week who is actually enlisting

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound with no documented funnel conversion benchmarks (§6). We have no stated expectation for application-to-sign or sign-to-retain ratios, making it impossible to forecast growth or hiring needs. This competitor has explicit numbers to plan against.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish Palm's own funnel conversion benchmarks by tracking applicants → signed → active → retained across a 90-day window. Even rough numbers (e.g. from Josh's current DM outreach) would let us set realistic pipeline targets and identify where the funnel leaks most.

new_findings.title: Use Facebook + Instagram DM outreach as primary dual-channel model sourcing

variants.claim: Facebook and Instagram together are the most effective channels for sourcing models, with Instagram DM scouting offering the added benefit of pre-qualifying candidates by their existing follower count and OF presence.
variants.what_they_do: They actively scout Instagram for women who already have a following and/or an OnlyFans — these candidates are higher-signal because their content appetite and audience fit is already demonstrated. Facebook is used in parallel for broader reach. They send cold DMs on Instagram as the primary contact method.

sources.quote: scouting on Instagram or sending DMs on Insta, right? This is basically the best way because it will make you help select the models you want to work with

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently relies on Josh's outbound DMs and referrals with no documented channel split or qualification criteria (§6). We do not appear to have a systematic Facebook acquisition effort running alongside Instagram.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add Facebook as a second sourcing channel alongside Instagram. Define a minimum-follower threshold and OF-presence flag as qualification criteria for cold DM outreach — this replicates their pre-qualification logic and should improve sign-to-retain ratio.

new_findings.title: Assign a geo-matched mobile proxy per OF account to prevent IP-based blocks at scale

variants.claim: Running multiple OnlyFans accounts from a single IP address will cause account blocks as you scale, so each account must use a mobile proxy matching the model's geographic location.
variants.what_they_do: Each creator's OnlyFans login is accessed through a mobile proxy set to the region the model is based in. This prevents the platform from flagging multiple accounts originating from the same IP. The model herself creates the account (for ID verification and bank details) and then hands over login credentials to the agency.

sources.quote: make sure that you use a mobile proxy with the area where she is from

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a hardware-isolated proxy/SIM setup for AI persona accounts (§3/§4) but no documented equivalent for real-creator OF account management. If Palm manages multiple real creators' OF logins, this gap is a live platform-risk exposure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a mobile proxy per real-creator OF account, geo-matched to each creator's location, using the same proxy-management discipline Palm already applies to AI accounts. Document this in a real-creator account-management SOP.

new_findings.title: Never mention 'OnlyFans' by name on social — use a link-in-bio tool instead

variants.claim: Writing the word 'OnlyFans' on Instagram or other social platforms triggers shadowbanning, making accounts effectively worthless; use a Linktree or equivalent redirect instead.
variants.what_they_do: All social accounts avoid the term 'OnlyFans' entirely in captions, bios, and comments. They route followers to OF via a free Linktree link-in-bio, keeping the social account clean of flagged keywords.

sources.quote: never use the word only fans on your social media accounts. They will shadow ban you if you do it and your accounts become worthless

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Beacons as a link-in-bio tool for AI personas (§3). It is not explicitly documented whether real-creator social accounts follow the same no-OF-mention rule. This is a basic hygiene policy that should be formalized for all Palm-managed accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a content policy rule across all Palm-managed social accounts (real and AI): never use 'OnlyFans' in text; always route via Beacons/Linktree. Add this as a checklist item in the editor approval step (§1, step 8) and the creator onboarding form (§6).

new_findings.topic: OF subscription vs PPV content tiering
new_findings.title: Tier OF content: subscription vault slightly better than free social; reserve best for PPV chat

variants.claim: Subscription content must be noticeably better than free social content to justify the sub, but must not reveal the creator's full range — that depth is reserved for PPV sold in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: They operate a three-tier content ladder: (1) free social teaser, (2) subscription vault that is 'slightly better' than social but deliberately incomplete, (3) explicit PPV sold during chat. Reels on social are kept to 5–10 seconds for best algorithmic performance. Background quality is emphasized as a trust signal, with American-aesthetic backgrounds used since the primary customer base is US-based.

sources.quote: what is very important is to make sure that what they get in the subscription is slightly better than what they get on the free social media accounts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on social content production (§1) but has a documented thin spot on OF vault/PPV content strategy (§2). This competitor's three-tier model gives a concrete framework Palm currently lacks. The social reel length guideline (5–10 seconds) is also not codified in Palm's pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a formal three-tier content policy for each Palm creator: social teaser → subscription vault → PPV/chat exclusive. Add reel duration guidance (5–10 seconds) to the content pipeline brief (§1). Work with the external chat team (§8) to ensure PPV sequencing aligns with this ladder.

new_findings.title: Escalate PPV content soft-to-hard to maximize purchase frequency per fan

variants.claim: Sending explicit content too early (e.g. the most extreme acts in the first message) destroys repeat-purchase motivation — always escalate gradually from soft to hard to extend the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a soft-to-hard content escalation: early PPV is tame/suggestive; progressively more explicit content is unlocked as the fan continues to purchase. The underlying logic is to keep the fan always feeling there is 'more to unlock,' extending the revenue relationship as long as possible before they disengage.

sources.quote: sell from soft to hard. It's super important

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team with no internal SOP (§8). This soft-to-hard escalation framework is a high-value build-toward tactic for Palm's planned in-house chat team and AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture soft-to-hard escalation as a core principle in the future Palm chatter SOP. Define 3–5 content 'tiers' per creator (e.g. teasing → topless → explicit acts) and script the upsell transition between each tier. Share this framework with the current external chat team as a quality guideline while in-house capability is built.

new_findings.title: Use mass DMs only to open conversations, never to push offers directly

variants.claim: Mass DMs sent as direct sales offers perform poorly; they work best exclusively as conversation-starters, which then lead to individualized upsells that earn far more.
variants.what_they_do: Mass DMs are used solely to initiate engagement (e.g. a simple personal-feeling opener). Once a fan responds and a conversation is live, the chatter pivots to individualized PPV sales. They explicitly avoid blasting offers via mass DM, framing it as '10x more money' to sell through conversation vs. cold offers. They also advise reading whether the fan is in a context where he can engage (not at dinner, not busy) before pushing a sale.

sources.quote: Mass DMs is a really important tool to get a conversation started and this should be the only thing that you want to use it for

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8). This mass-DM-as-opener-only rule is a concrete, testable tactic that Palm's in-house/AI chat roadmap should adopt. It also has implications for how Palm might use OF's mass-message feature for real creators today — worth validating with the current external team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the current external chat team on this principle and confirm their mass-DM usage aligns (opener only, not offer blast). Encode 'mass DM = conversation opener only' as a standing rule in the future Palm chatter playbook and AI chat system prompts.

new_findings.title: Record all posting and chatting actions from day one to generate optimization data

variants.claim: Failing to log what you post and what you do in chat means you cannot identify what is actually working, wasting time and causing you to repeat mistakes indefinitely.
variants.what_they_do: They track all posting activity and chat interactions in spreadsheets (Excel templates), maintaining a consistent posting schedule for at least one month without changes before evaluating results. They also follow other creators' accounts deliberately to train the algorithm to surface their content to relevant audiences.

sources.quote: Record everything you do. Now, this gives you valuable feedback on how to proceed in the future. I made the mistake by not doing that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature content pipeline with Airtable tracking for social posts (§1) and whale-hunting/fan CRM data collection underway (§9). However, a real-creator posting cadence/frequency standard is flagged as a GAP (§1), and no chat-performance logging exists internally (§8 outsourced). The competitor's discipline of holding a schedule constant for ≥1 month before changing it is a useful operational norm Palm should codify.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a policy to Palm's content pipeline: once a posting schedule is set for a creator, hold it unchanged for a minimum of 4 weeks before any cadence adjustment, and document the rationale for any change. For the chat roadmap, design logging into the chatter workflow from day one — even simple metrics (messages sent, PPV offers made, conversion rate) will be invaluable for optimization.

new_findings.topic: AI-generated marketing content to reduce creator dependency
new_findings.title: Use AI-generated social/marketing content to replace or supplement creator-produced content

variants.claim: AI-generated marketing content removes the need for the creator to be a skilled content producer, making the agency's output independent of creator talent.
variants.what_they_do: Jason's agency uses AI to generate a significant portion of its marketing content (top-of-funnel social content). This is distinct from OF vault/PPV content — it is the promotional material used to drive traffic. By generating this content internally with AI, the agency no longer requires the creator to film high-quality social content consistently, reducing friction and creator-side dependencies.

sources.quote: we also use AI to generate a lot of the marketing content that we use, which again removes a lot of the friction from the model side in needing to be talented, in needing to execute at a high level, in needing to be on time.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already produces AI social content for some real creators and runs full AI personas (§0, §3). Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and agency-run, which partially achieves the same friction-reduction. However, using AI to generate marketing content for real creator accounts (rather than just AI persona accounts) is not formally documented as a tactic. This is a meaningful overlap area — Palm is closer to this than most agencies.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally document and expand the use of AI-generated marketing assets for real creator accounts as part of the BFAM-equivalent workflow. Specifically: identify which content types for real creators can be AI-generated (text posts, captions, carousel graphics, voiceover reels) vs. must-be-creator-filmed (face content). Build this into the content pipeline (§1) as a content-type routing decision.

new_findings.title: Retain creators by delivering fast zero-to-revenue results with minimal creator effort required

variants.claim: Creators stay and remain satisfied when the agency takes them from $0 to thousands per month with only a few hours of work per week on their side.
variants.what_they_do: Jason explicitly frames low creator effort + fast revenue as the core creator retention mechanism. By removing friction (creators only upload to a drive, agency does everything else), creators work just a few hours per week. Combined with real revenue results fast (e.g., $12K month one), creator churn is low because the value proposition is undeniable. He positions this as why he can 'keep creators incredibly happy.'

sources.quote: we keep our creators incredibly happy because we take them from earning zero dollars to multiple thousands of dollars every single month with barely any input from their side, working just a few hours per week.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented retention or churn strategy (§9 🔲 GAP). Palm's system does reduce creator friction (§1 pipeline, agency-run posting), but there is no written framework for measuring creator satisfaction, tracking churn signals, or running a success cadence with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's creator retention playbook: (1) set explicit effort expectations at onboarding ('you work X hours/week, we do the rest'); (2) establish a 30/60/90 day revenue milestone check-in cadence; (3) track creator churn signals in Airtable (missed uploads, reduced responsiveness, revenue plateau complaints). Use fast early results as the primary retention lever and document the expected revenue ramp per creator tier.

new_findings.title: Six-step ad script for qualified creator leads

variants.claim: A repeatable six-step ad script (attention, key benefit, without-the-pain, proof, time-sensitive offer, downside of inaction) structures creative for conversion rather than ad-hoc messaging.
variants.what_they_do: Uses a fixed 6-step structure with one chosen angle (speed, risk-reduction, or ease); language must be coherent from ad to VSL to funnel to call and matched to a specific avatar (e.g. creators already doing 10k/mo) to avoid attracting low-quality opportunity-seekers.

sources.quote: the six-step ad script looks like this... number one, attention... number six is the downside of inaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: No acquisition messaging framework or avatar definition exists at Palm (§6 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 6-step script and define a concrete creator avatar (revenue band, follower count) so Palm outreach and any future content speaks one coherent, qualifying message.

new_findings.title: Hybrid keeps OnlyFans trust vs full-AI Fanvue conversion loss

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.
